response_texts:
Set  S_{k}=a_{1}^{2}+a_{2}^{2}+\dots +a_{k}^{2}\qquad(1\le k\le n),
and put  

c_{k}=\sqrt{1-\dfrac{1}{16^{k}}}\;(= \sqrt{1-4^{-2k}}),\qquad   
w_{k}=
\begin{cases}
c_{k}&(k<n),\\[2mm]
\dfrac43\,c_{n}&(k=n).
\end{cases}

We have to minimise  

F(a_{1},\dots ,a_{n})=\sum_{k=1}^{n}w_{k}\sqrt{S_{k}}\qquad\text{subject to }
a_{1}+a_{2}+ \dots +a_{n}=1.                                     (1)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  The critical–point equations
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Because each map \(x\mapsto\sqrt{x}\) is convex and increasing, the
function \(F\) is convex in \((a_{1},\dots ,a_{n})\); hence every
stationary point of (1) yields the global minimum.

Introduce the Lagrange multiplier \(\lambda\)
\[
\Phi =F+\lambda\Bigl(a_{1}+a_{2}+\dots +a_{n}-1\Bigr).
\]

For every \(j\;(1\le j\le n)\),

\[
0=\frac{\partial\Phi}{\partial a_{j}}
   =a_{j}\sum_{k=j}^{\,n}\frac{w_{k}}{\sqrt{S_{k}}}+\lambda .
\]
Thus there is a constant \(C=-\lambda>0\) such that  

a_{j}\sum_{k=j}^{n}\frac{w_{k}}{\sqrt{S_{k}}}=C\qquad(1\le j\le n). \tag{2}

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  Solving (2)
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Put  

T_{j}=\sum_{k=j}^{n}\frac{w_{k}}{\sqrt{S_{k}}}.
\quad\text{Then}\quad a_{j}T_{j}=C\;(=a_{j+1}T_{j+1}).     (3)

Hence

\[
\frac{a_{j+1}}{a_{j}}
     =\frac{T_{j}}{T_{j+1}}
     =\frac{w_{j}}{\sqrt{S_{j}}}\Big/\frac{w_{j+1}}{\sqrt{S_{j+1}}}
     =\frac{c_{j}}{c_{j+1}}\,
       \frac{\sqrt{S_{j+1}}}{\sqrt{S_{j}}}.
\]

Because \(S_{j+1}=S_{j}+a_{j+1}^{2}\),
relation (3) forces  

\[
\frac{a_{j+1}}{a_{j}}=4\qquad (1\le j\le n-1).
\]

So the minimiser must be a geometric progression

\[
a_{1}:a_{2}:\dots :a_{n}=1:4:4^{2}:\dots :4^{\,n-1}.           (4)
\]

------------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  Determining the minimising vector
------------------------------------------------------------------------
Let \(a_{1}=t\).  From (4) and the constraint \(a_{1}+a_{2}+\dots +a_{n}=1\),

\[
t\bigl(1+4+4^{2}+\dots +4^{\,n-1}\bigr)=1
\;\Longrightarrow\;
t=\frac{3}{4^{\,n}-1}.
\]

Hence  

a_{k}=t\cdot4^{\,k-1}= \frac{3\cdot4^{\,k-1}}{4^{\,n}-1}\qquad(1\le k\le n). \tag{5}

------------------------------------------------------------------------
4.  The value of F at the critical point
------------------------------------------------------------------------
For this vector we have  

\[
S_{k}=t^{2}\frac{4^{\,2k}-1}{4^{2}-1}=t^{2}\frac{4^{\,2k}-1}{15},
\qquad
\sqrt{S_{k}}=\frac{t\,4^{\,k}}{\sqrt{15}}\,c_{k}.
\]

Therefore  

\[
b_{k}=c_{k}\sqrt{S_{k}}
     =\frac{t\,4^{\,k}}{\sqrt{15}}\,c_{k}^{2}
     =\frac{t}{\sqrt{15}}\bigl(4^{\,k}-4^{-k}\bigr)
      \qquad(1\le k\le n).
\]

Hence  

\[
\begin{aligned}
F &=\sum_{k=1}^{n-1}b_{k}+\frac43\,b_{n}\\
  &=\frac{t}{\sqrt{15}}
     \Biggl[\sum_{k=1}^{n-1}\bigl(4^{\,k}-4^{-k}\bigr)
            +\frac43\bigl(4^{\,n}-4^{-n}\bigr)\Biggr] \\[2mm]
  &=\frac{t}{3\sqrt{15}}
     \Bigl[\,4^{\,n}-5+4^{-(n-1)}+4^{\,n+1}-4\cdot4^{-n}\Bigr].
\end{aligned}
\]

Because \(4^{-(n-1)}-4\cdot4^{-n}=0\), the bracket equals \(5(4^{\,n}-1)\);
with \(t=3/(4^{\,n}-1)\) this becomes  

\[
F=\frac{5}{\sqrt{15}}=\frac{\sqrt{15}}{3}.
\]

------------------------------------------------------------------------
5.  Minimality
------------------------------------------------------------------------
Since \(F\) is convex, the only stationary point given by (5)
is the global minimiser, and the value just computed is the minimal
value.  Taking all \(a_{k}\) of the opposite sign gives the same value,
so these two vectors are the complete set of minimisers.

------------------------------------------------------------------------
6.  Result
------------------------------------------------------------------------
The minimal value is independent of \(n\;(n\ge 2)\):

\[
\boxed{\displaystyle \frac{\sqrt{15}}{3}}
\]

and it is attained for  

\[
(a_{1},a_{2},\dots ,a_{n})
     =\frac{3}{4^{\,n}-1}\;(1,4,4^{2},\dots ,4^{\,n-1})
\quad\text{or its negative.}
\]

Write

 cₖ = √(1-16⁻ᵏ) (1 ≤ k ≤ n–1),  cₙ = 4⁄3·√(1-16⁻ⁿ)

and

 S = Σ_{k=1}^{n} cₖ √(a₁²+···+a_k²),    a₁+···+a_n = 1.             (1)

Step 1.  Reduction to the case n = 2  
Because the sequence {cₖ} is strictly increasing, moving any positive a_j
to the right (that is, increasing an a_i with a larger coefficient and
decreasing one with a smaller coefficient) makes S larger.  
Hence, for the minimum we may assume that at most the last two variables are
non–zero.  
Consequently it is sufficient to minimise S for n = 2.

Step 2.  Minimisation for n = 2  
Put b₁ = √15 /4 ,  b₂ = 4⁄3·√(1-16⁻²)=√255 /12 .  
Let a₁ = t, a₂ = 1–t (0 ≤ t ≤ 1).  Then

 S(t) = b₁ t + b₂ √(t²+(1-t)²)                                (2)

Differentiate:

 S′(t)= b₁ + b₂ (4t-2)/(2√(2t²-2t+1)),

set S′(t)=0 and solve.  Denoting r = (b₁/b₂)² we obtain

 (4–2r)t² + (–4+2r)t + (1–r)=0,

whose two roots are

 t = ½ ± ½√(r/(2–r)).

Because b₁ < b₂ (so r<1), the smaller root gives the minimum:

 t = ½ – ½√(r/(2–r)).                                        (3)

For b₁² = 15/16 and b₂² = 255/144 we have r = (15/16)/(255/144)=9/17,
hence √(r/(2–r)) = √((9/17)/(25/17)) = 3/5.  
Therefore t = 1/5 and a₂ = 4/5.

Step 3.  The minimum value  
Insert t = 1/5 in (2):

 S_min = (√15/4)(1/5) + (√255/12)√((1/5)²+(4/5)²)  
     = √15 /20 + (√255/12)(√17/5)  
     = √15 /20 + √(255·17) /60  
     = √15 /20 + √(15·17²)/60  
     = √15 /20 + 17√15 /60  
     = √15 (1/20 + 17/60)  
     = √15 (3/60 +17/60)  
     = √15 ·20/60 = √15 /3.                                (4)

Step 4.  The general case  
For n ≥ 3 keep a₁ = 1/5, a₂ = 4/5, a₃ = ··· = a_n = 0.  
Because every additional term in (1) is non–negative and the two first
terms equal those in (4), any S for n ≥ 3 is not smaller than √15 /3.  

Hence the minimum in (1) is already attained for n = 2,
with (a₁,a₂) = (1/5, 4/5), and its value is

\boxed{\dfrac{\sqrt{15}}{3}}

Set  

d_k=\sqrt{1-\dfrac1{16^{\,k}}}\qquad (k=1,2,\dots ,n-1),\qquad   
d_n=\frac43\sqrt{1-\dfrac1{16^{\,n}}},\qquad   
S=\sum_{k=1}^{n}d_k\sqrt{a_1^{2}+a_2^{2}+\dots +a_k^{2}},\qquad   
a_1+\dots +a_n=1.  

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  The case  n = 2  

Write a_1=x , a_2 = 1-x \,(0\le x\le 1).  Then  

S(x)=d_1x+d_2\sqrt{x^{2}+(1-x)^2}
     =\frac{\sqrt{15}}4\,x+\frac{\sqrt{255}}{12}\sqrt{2x^{2}-2x+1}.  

Differentiating and equating to 0 yields  

x=\frac15,\qquad 1-x=\frac45.  

Hence  

S_{\min}=S\!\left(\frac15\right)
        =\frac{\sqrt{15}}4\cdot\frac15+\frac{\sqrt{255}}{12}\cdot\frac{\sqrt{17}}5
        =\frac{\sqrt{15}}3.  

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  The general case  n ≥ 3  

Put  

A_k=\sqrt{a_1^{2}+a_2^{2}+\dots +a_k^{2}},\qquad k=1,2,\dots ,n.  

Because  A_k\le A_n  and  d_k<d_n\;(k<n)  we have  

S=d_1A_1+d_2A_2+\dots +d_{n-1}A_{\,n-1}+d_nA_n
  \ge d_1A_1+d_nA_n
  =\frac{\sqrt{15}}4\,A_1+\frac43\!\sqrt{1-\frac1{16^{\,n}}}\,A_n
  >\frac{\sqrt{15}}4\,A_1+\frac{\sqrt{255}}{12}\,A_n.  

Now regard only the first two coordinates (keep the others fixed).  
With  

x=A_1,\qquad y=\sqrt{A_n^{2}-A_1^{2}},\qquad x^{2}+y^{2}=A_n^{2},  

the right‐hand side has the same form as in the case n=2, and its
minimum is  √15 /3, attained when  

x=\frac15,\qquad y=\frac45,\qquad\Longrightarrow\quad
A_n=1,\;A_1=\frac15.  

Therefore  

S_{\min}\ge\frac{\sqrt{15}}3.  

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  Attainment of the lower bound  

For n=2 choose (a_1,a_2)=(\tfrac15,\tfrac45);  
then every step above is an equality and  

S_{\min}= \dfrac{\sqrt{15}}3.  

For n>2 take  

(a_1,a_2)=(\tfrac15,\tfrac45),\qquad a_3=\dots =a_n=0;  

all inequalities again turn to equalities, so the same minimum is
reached.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Minimum value  

In every case n≥2 the least possible value of  

b_1+b_2+\dots +b_{n-1}+\frac43\,b_{\,n}  

equals  

\boxed{\dfrac{\sqrt{15}}{3}}

Write  

c_{k}= \sqrt{1-\dfrac1{16^{\,k}}}\qquad (k\ge 1),\qquad   
d_{k}=c_{k}\;(k<n),\; d_{n}=\dfrac43\,c_{n} .  

For a_{1},\dots ,a_{n}\,(a_{i}\ge 0), put  

S_{k}=a_{1}^{2}+a_{2}^{2}+\,\dots +\,a_{k}^{2}\qquad(1\le k\le n).  

The expression to be minimised is  

F(a_{1},\dots ,a_{n})=\sum_{k=1}^{n} d_{k}\sqrt{S_{k}}\qquad\text{subject to }a_{1}+a_{2}+\dots +a_{n}=1.  

1.  The optimum has at most two non–zero coordinates.  
    Indeed, assume three of the a_{i}’s, say a_{p},a_{q},a_{r}\;(p<q<r), are
    positive.  Vary (a_{q},a_{r}) along the line a_{q}+a_{r}=const while the
    other coordinates are fixed.  
    Because  

        \frac{\partial F}{\partial a_{q}}
        =a_{q}\Bigl(\sum_{k=q}^{n}\frac{d_{k}}{\sqrt{S_{k}}}\Bigr),\qquad
        \frac{\partial F}{\partial a_{r}}
        =a_{r}\Bigl(\sum_{k=r}^{n}\frac{d_{k}}{\sqrt{S_{k}}}\Bigr),

    and because the bracket in the first derivative is strictly larger than
    the one in the second (d_{k} is non-decreasing and S_{k} is
    non-decreasing), we can diminish F by transferring a small amount from
    a_{q} to a_{r}.  
    Repeating this procedure shows that in any minimiser all but the first two
    coordinates must be zero.  Hence we may (and do) put n=2.

2.  So let a_{1}=x,\;a_{2}=1-x\;(0\le x\le 1).  Then  

    b_{1}=c_{1}x,\qquad b_{2}=c_{2}\sqrt{x^{2}+(1-x)^{2}} ,
    and the quantity to minimise is  

        F_{2}(x)=c_{1}x+\frac43\,c_{2}\sqrt{\,x^{2}+(1-x)^{2}} ,
        \qquad 0\le x\le 1,

    where  

        c_{1}=\sqrt{\frac{15}{16}}=\frac{\sqrt{15}}4,\qquad
        c_{2}=\sqrt{\frac{255}{256}}=\frac{\sqrt{255}}{16} .

3.  Differentiate:

        F_{2}'(x)=c_{1}+
        \frac43\,c_{2}\,\frac{2x-1}{\sqrt{2x^{2}-2x+1}}.

    The critical points satisfy  

        (2x-1)^{2}=\frac{c_{1}^{2}}{(\tfrac43 c_{2})^{2}}\,
                   (2x^{2}-2x+1)
        \quad\Longrightarrow\quad
        25x^{2}-25x+4=0 ,
        whence  x=\frac15\;(=0.2)\ \text{or}\ x=\frac45.

    The second root gives a maximum, the first a minimum.  Therefore

        a_{1}=\frac15,\;a_{2}=\frac45.

4.  The minimum value is then  

        F_{\min}=c_{1}\cdot\frac15
                 +\frac43\,c_{2}\cdot
                   \sqrt{\Bigl(\frac15\Bigr)^{2}+\Bigl(\frac45\Bigr)^{2}}
               =\frac{\sqrt{15}}{20}
                +\frac{\sqrt{255}}{12}\cdot\frac{\sqrt{17}}5
               =\frac{3\sqrt{15}+17\sqrt{15}}{60}
               =\frac{\sqrt{15}}{3} .

5.  Because every admissible (a_{1},\dots ,a_{n}) can be reduced (without
    increasing the objective function) to a pair (a_{1},a_{2}) with the same
    sum, the value obtained in step 4 is the global minimum for every
    n≥2.

Hence the required minimum equals  

\boxed{\dfrac{\sqrt{15}}{3}}

Put  

s_k=\sqrt{1-\dfrac1{16^{\,k}}}\qquad(k\ge 1),\hspace{1cm}
w_k=
\begin{cases}
s_k &(1\le k\le n-1)\\[4pt]
\dfrac43\,s_n &(k=n).
\end{cases}

With c_k=\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{k}a_i^{2}\;(k\ge 1) we have

b_k=w_k\sqrt{c_k},\qquad      
S:=\sum_{k=1}^{n-1}b_k+\frac43\,b_n
      =\sum_{k=1}^{n}w_k\sqrt{c_k},
\qquad
c_1\le c_2\le\dots\le c_n.                                       (1)

------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  Only the last two $a_i$’s are needed
------------------------------------------------------------------
For $k\le n-2$ the quantity $a_k^{2}$ is contained in every
$c_j\;(j\ge k)$, hence in every $\sqrt{c_j}$ that appears in $S$;
if the same square is moved to the position $n-1$ it is still
present in $c_{n-1},c_n$ but disappears from
$c_1,\ldots ,c_{n-2}$, so $S$ does not increase.
Repeating this step we may assume that  

a_1=\dots =a_{n-2}=0,\qquad a_{n-1}=x,\qquad a_n=1-x,\qquad 0\le x\le1.  (2)

With (2)  

c_{n-1}=x^{2},\quad c_n=x^{2}+(1-x)^{2}=1-2x+2x^{2}.               (3)

All earlier $c_k$’s equal $x^{2}$, hence by (1)

S(x)=w_{n-1}x+w_n\sqrt{1-2x+2x^{2}},\qquad 0\le x\le1.            (4)

------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  The best choice of $x$
------------------------------------------------------------------
Put  

p=w_{n-1}=s_{\,n-1},\qquad q=w_n=\frac43\,s_n,\qquad r=\frac{p}{q}. 

Using (4) one finds  

S'(x)=p+q\;\frac{-2+4x}{2\sqrt{1-2x+2x^{2}}}=0
\Longleftrightarrow
\frac{p}{q}=\frac{1-2x}{\sqrt{1-2x+2x^{2}}}.                     (5)

Writing $t=1-2x$ ($0<t\le 1$), equation (5) becomes  

r=\dfrac{t\sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{1+t^{2}}}\quad (0<t\le 1)              (6)

and gives  

t=\dfrac{r}{\sqrt{\,2-r^{2}}},\qquad         
x=\dfrac{1-t}{2}
         =\frac12\Bigl(1-\frac{r}{\sqrt{\,2-r^{2}}}\Bigr).        (7)

------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  The minimum value of $S$
------------------------------------------------------------------
Substituting (7) in (4) yields after a little algebra

S_{\min}=q\!\left[\,
              \frac{r}{2}+
              \frac{1-\dfrac{r^{2}}{2}}{\sqrt{\,2-r^{2}}}\right]
        =q\!\left[\,
              \frac{3u}{8}+\frac{\sqrt{\,32-9u^{2}}}{8}\right]     (8)

where  

u=\frac{s_{\,n-1}}{s_n},\qquad r=\frac34\,u.                      (9)

Because $q=\dfrac43\,s_n$, (8) becomes

S_{\min}(n)=
\frac{s_n}{6}\Bigl(3u+\sqrt{\,32-9u^{2}}\Bigr)                   (10)

with $u$ given by (9).

------------------------------------------------------------------
4.  Writing the answer in $n$
------------------------------------------------------------------
Since  
u^{2}= \dfrac{1-\;16^{-(n-1)}}{1-\;16^{-n}},\qquad
s_n=\sqrt{1-16^{-n}},                                            (11)

formula (10) gives the required minimum:

S_{\min}(n)=
\frac{\sqrt{\,1-16^{-n}}}{6}\!
\left(
 3\sqrt{\frac{1-16^{-(n-1)}}{1-16^{-n}}}+
 \sqrt{\,32-9\frac{1-16^{-(n-1)}}{1-16^{-n}}}
\right).                                                         (12)

------------------------------------------------------------------
5.  Check for $n=2$
------------------------------------------------------------------
For $n=2$ formula (12) yields

S_{\min}(2)=\frac{1}{3}\sqrt{15},

the value obtained directly from $a_1=\tfrac15,\;a_2=\tfrac45$,
confirming the formula.

Thus for every integer $n\ge 2$ the least possible value of  

b_1+b_2+\dots+b_{\,n-1}+\dfrac43\,b_n

is given by (12), and it is attained when  

a_1=\dots =a_{n-2}=0,\;
a_{n-1}=\dfrac12\!\left(1-\dfrac{r}{\sqrt{2-r^{2}}}\right),\;
a_n=\dfrac12\!\left(1+\dfrac{r}{\sqrt{2-r^{2}}}\right)
\quad\bigl(r=\tfrac34\,u\bigr).

\boxed{%
\displaystyle
\min S=
\frac{\sqrt{1-16^{-n}}}{6}\!
\left(
 3\sqrt{\frac{1-16^{-(n-1)}}{1-16^{-n}}}+
 \sqrt{\,32-9\frac{1-16^{-(n-1)}}{1-16^{-n}}}
\right)
}

Step 1.  Notation  
Put  
 S₁ = a₁ , S₂ = √(a₁²+a₂²) , … , Sₖ = √(a₁²+…+aₖ²) (1≤k≤n).  
Write  
 cₖ = √(1−16^{−k}) (k≥1).  

The quantity to be minimised is  

 F(a₁,…,a_n)=∑_{k=1}^{n-1} cₖ Sₖ+\frac43 c_n S_n.   (∗)

Notice that c₁<c₂<…<c_n and cₖ≥c₂ for every k≥2.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 2.  Reducing the problem to the case n = 2  
Split (∗) as  

 F = c₁|a₁| + [c₂S₂] + ∑_{k=3}^{n-1}cₖSₖ + \frac43 c_n S_n.  

Because cₖ≥c₂ and Sₖ≥S₂ (monotonicity of both sequences), we have for k≥3  

 cₖSₖ ≥ c₂S₂.   

Hence  

 ∑_{k=3}^{n-1}cₖSₖ + \frac43 c_n S_n
  ≥ (n-3)c₂S₂ + \frac43 c₂S₂
  ≥ \frac13 c₂S₂  (for every n≥2).

Therefore  

 F ≥ c₁|a₁| + \frac43 c₂S₂ − \frac13 c₂S₂
  = c₁|a₁| + \frac43 c₂S₂. (1)

Inequality (1) shows that for the purpose of getting the minimum of F we may assume n = 2 and minimise  

 G(x)=c₁|x|+\frac43 c₂√(2x²−2x+1),  x=a₁ (a₂=1−x). (2)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 3.  Exact values of the constants  
c₁ = √(15/16)= √15/4, c₂ = √(255/256)= √255/16 = √(15·17)/16.  
Consequently  

 c₁/c₂ = (√15/4)/(√(15·17)/16)=4/√17.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 4.  Minimising G(x)  
Because the expression in (2) is even with respect to x→1−x, we may restrict to 0≤x≤½ and drop the absolute value.

Differentiate:

 G′(x)=c₁ + \frac43 c₂· (2x−1)/√(2x²−2x+1).

Set G′(x)=0:

 c₁ + \frac43 c₂· (2x−1)/√(2x²−2x+1)=0  
⇔ 1−2x = \frac34·(c₁/c₂) √(2x²−2x+1)  
⇔ 1−2x = \frac3{√17} √(2x²−2x+1).

Squaring and simplifying yields  

 25x²−25x+4=0 ⇒ x = 1/5  (or 4/5, the symmetric point).

Thus the minimiser in the interval [0,1] is x=1/5.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 5.  Minimum value
At x = 1/5 we have  

 S₁ = 1/5, S₂ = √(17)/5.

G(1/5) = c₁·\frac15 + \frac43 c₂·\frac{√17}{5}  
     = \frac{√15}{20} + \frac43 ·\frac{√{15·17}}{16}·\frac{√17}{5}  
     = \frac{√15}{20} + \frac{17√15}{60}  
     = \frac{√15}{3}.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 6.  Conclusion  
Inequality (1) gives F ≥ √15/3 for every choice of n≥2 and of (a₁,…,a_n), while the choice

 n = 2, a₁ = 1/5, a₂ = 4/5

makes the inequality an equality. Therefore

 min F = √15/3.

\boxed{\dfrac{\sqrt{15}}{3}}

Step 1.  Reformulation  

For 1 ≤ k ≤ n put  
T_k=\sqrt{a_{1}^{2}+a_{2}^{2}+\dots +a_{k}^{2}},  
β_k=\sqrt{1-\dfrac1{16^{k}}}\;(=:\alpha_k).  

Let  

S(a_1,\dots ,a_n)=\sum_{k=1}^{n-1}\beta_kT_k+\frac43\,\beta_nT_n  
(the expression we have to minimise)  
under the linear constraint  

a_1+a_2+\dots +a_n=1.                                      (1)

Because T_k is a Euclidean norm, each term β_kT_k is a convex
function of (a_1,\dots ,a_k); hence S is convex in the whole vector
(a_1,\dots ,a_n).  
Therefore every stationary point of S subject to (1) is a global
minimum.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 2.  Lagrange equations  

Set  

w_k=\beta_k\;(k<n),  w_n=\frac43\,\beta_n .

Introduce the Lagrange function  

L=S-\lambda\!\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n}a_i-1\right).

For j=1,\dots ,n  

∂L/∂a_j = a_j\sum_{k=j}^{n}\frac{w_k}{T_k}-\lambda =0.        (2)

So there is a constant λ with  

a_j\sum_{k=j}^{n}\frac{w_k}{T_k}=λ  (j=1,\dots ,n).          (3)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 3.  Searching a solution of (3)

Let us try a geometric progression

a_{k+1}=4a_k (k=1,\dots ,n-1).                              (4)

Write a_k=c\,4^{k-1} with a constant c>0.  
Because of (1)

1=\sum_{k=1}^{n}a_k=c\sum_{k=1}^{n}4^{k-1}=c\frac{4^{n}-1}{3},
hence  

c=\frac{3}{4^{n}-1}.                                         (5)

With (4)–(5) we have

T_k=\sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{k}a_i^{2}}
    =c\sqrt{\frac{16^{k}-1}{15}},                            (6)

and for k<n  

\frac{w_k}{T_k}=\frac{\sqrt{1-16^{-k}}}{T_k}
              =\frac{\sqrt{16^{k}-1}}{4^{k}T_k}
              =\frac{\sqrt{15}}{c\,4^{k}},                  (7)

while for k=n  

\frac{w_n}{T_n}=\frac{\tfrac43\sqrt{1-16^{-n}}}{T_n}
              =\frac{\sqrt{15}}{c}\cdot\frac{4}{3\;4^{\,n}}. (8)

Using (7)–(8)

\sum_{k=j}^{n}\frac{w_k}{T_k}
      =\frac{\sqrt{15}}{c}\Bigl[\frac{4}{3}\!\sum_{k=j}^{n-1}\!4^{-k}
                                 +\frac{4}{3}\,4^{-n}\Bigr]
      =\frac{\sqrt{15}}{c}\,\frac{4}{3}\,4^{1-j}.            (9)

Therefore, with a_j=c\,4^{j-1},

a_j\sum_{k=j}^{n}\frac{w_k}{T_k}
   =\bigl(c4^{\,j-1}\bigr)\Bigl(\frac{\sqrt{15}}{c}\,
                                 \frac{4}{3}\,4^{1-j}\Bigr)
   =\frac{\sqrt{15}}{3}\;=:λ,                                (10)

which is independent of j; thus (3) is satisfied.  
Consequently the vector (4)–(5) is a stationary point, hence a global
minimiser.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 4.  The minimum value

Using (4)–(6)

β_kT_k=\frac{c}{\sqrt{15}}\bigl(4^k-4^{-k}\bigr) (k<n),  
β_nT_n=\frac{c}{\sqrt{15}}\bigl(4^n-4^{-n}\bigr).

Hence  

S=\frac{c}{\sqrt{15}}\Bigl[
        \sum_{k=1}^{n-1}\!(4^k-4^{-k})
        +\frac43\,(4^n-4^{-n})
      \Bigr].

A routine summation gives

∑_{k=1}^{n-1}(4^k-4^{-k})+\frac43(4^n-4^{-n})
      =\frac{5}{3}\,(4^n-1).

With c from (5) this yields

S_{\min}= \frac{3}{4^{n}-1}\cdot\frac{1}{\sqrt{15}}\,
          \frac{5}{3}\,(4^{\,n}-1)
        =\frac{\sqrt{15}}{3}.                               (11)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 5.  Equality case

Equality holds exactly for the sequence (4)–(5), i.e. for

a_k=\frac{3\cdot4^{\,k-1}}{4^{\,n}-1}\quad(k=1,\dots ,n),

which indeed satisfies a_{k+1}=4a_k and a_1+\dots +a_n=1.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Final answer  

The minimum value is  

\boxed{\dfrac{\sqrt{15}}{3}}

Let  

a₁+a₂+···+aₙ = 1  (n ≥ 2),                        
rₖ = √(a₁²+···+aₖ² )    (1 ≤ k ≤ n) ,   
cₖ = √(1 – 16^ –k ).

The quantity to be minimised is  

F =  c₁ r₁ + c₂ r₂ + … + c_{n-1} r_{n-1} + (4/3)cₙ rₙ .        (★)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  Reduction to the case n = 2
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Because r₁ ≤ r₂ ≤ … ≤ rₙ, and because the coefficients  
c₁ < c₂ < … < cₙ < (4/3)cₙ , any non–zero value put into a coordinate
aₖ with k ≥ 3 is multiplied at least by the positive number cₖ and at
the same time enlarges all the later radii r_j (j ≥ k), so it can only
increase F.  Consequently, in an optimal vector (a₁,…,aₙ) all the
coordinates except possibly the first two must be zero; i.e. the search
for the minimum of (★) can be restricted to

a₃ = … = aₙ = 0 , a₁+a₂ = 1 .                                (1)

With (1) we have r₁ = |a₁| and r₂ = |(a₁,a₂)| = √(a₁²+a₂²).  
Denote x = a₁ (–∞ < x < ∞); then a₂ = 1–x and (★) becomes

f(x) = c₁ |x| + (4/3)c₂ √(x² + (1–x)²).                     (2)

Because f(–x) = f(x), it is enough to consider x ≥ 0.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  Minimising f(x)
--------------------------------------------------------------------
For x ≥ 0 the modulus in (2) can be removed:

f(x) = c₁ x + (4/3)c₂ √(2x² – 2x + 1).

Write r(x) = √(2x² – 2x + 1).  
Then

f′(x) = c₁ + (4/3)c₂ (2x–1)/r(x).

Setting f′(x)=0 gives  

c₁ r(x) + (4/3)c₂ (1 – 2x) = 0.                             (3)

Putting the constants c₁ = √15 /4 and c₂ = √255 /16 into (3),
squaring and simplifying yield

25x² – 25x + 4 = 0,

whose solutions are x = 1/5 and x = 4/5; only x = 1/5 (< ½)
corresponds to a minimum.  Substituting x = 1/5 in (2) gives

r(1/5) = √17 /5 ,

f_min = (√15 /4)(1/5) + (4/3)(√255/16)(√17/5)
       = √15 /20 + √4335 /60
       = (3√15 + 17√15) / 60
       = √15 /3 .                                           (4)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  Optimal vector and attainment of the bound
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Take  
a₁ = 1/5 , a₂ = 4/5 , aₖ = 0 (k ≥ 3).

For this vector the value of the expression in the statement is exactly
√15 /3, so √15 /3 is attainable.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4.  Lower bound for arbitrary n
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Let (a₁,…,aₙ) be arbitrary with a₁+···+aₙ = 1.  
Put s = a₁, t = a₂ + … + aₙ = 1 – s.  
From the monotonicity of the radii we have

r₁ ≥ |s|, r₂ ≥ √(s²+t²) = r ,

and for k ≥ 2 the factors multiplying r_k in (★) are not smaller than
the coefficient of r₂ (namely c₂ for k = 2, 1 thereafter and finally
(4/3)cₙ > 1).  Hence  

F ≥ c₁|s| + (4/3)c₂ r ≥  min_{x∈ℝ} f(x)  (by the definition of f),

and by (4) the right-hand side equals √15 /3.  Therefore  

F ≥ √15 /3

for every admissible n and every real n-tuple (a₁,…,aₙ).  Equality
holds when n = 2 and (a₁,a₂) = (1/5,4/5).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
5.  Minimum value
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Thus the minimum of  

b₁ + b₂ + … + b_{n-1} + (4/3) bₙ

under the given conditions is the constant

\boxed{\dfrac{\sqrt{15}}{3}}.

Let  

d_k=\sqrt{1-\dfrac1{16^{\,k}}}\qquad (k=1,2,\dots ) ,\qquad   
s_k=\sqrt{a_1^{2}+a_2^{2}+\dots +a_k^{2}}\quad(1\le k\le n),
so that  
b_k=d_k\,s_k\;(1\le k\le n).

We have to minimise  

F=\sum_{k=1}^{n-1}b_k+\frac43 b_n
  =\sum_{k=1}^{n-1}d_k s_k+\frac43\,d_n s_n           (1)

under the single condition  

a_1+a_2+\dots +a_n=1.                                   (2)


------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  The optimum is reached with only two non–zero coordinates  
------------------------------------------------------------------

Introduce a Lagrange multiplier λ for (2):

L=\sum_{k=1}^{n-1}d_k s_k+\frac43d_n s_n-\lambda
        (a_1+a_2+\dots +a_n-1).

Because  

∂s_k/∂a_j=\dfrac{a_j}{s_k}\qquad(j\le k)

we obtain, for every j,

0=\frac{\partial L}{\partial a_j}
  =a_j\!\!\sum_{k=j}^{n}\!\frac{w_kd_k}{s_k}-\lambda ,
\quad w_k=\begin{cases}
1&(k<n)\\[2pt]4/3&(k=n).
\end{cases}

Hence

a_j=\dfrac{\lambda}{H_j},\qquad 
H_j=\sum_{k=j}^{n}\frac{w_kd_k}{s_k}.                 (3)

Because the sequence (s_k)_{k=1}^{n} is strictly increasing,
H_1>H_2>\dots >H_n, so (3) implies

a_1<a_2<\dots <a_n.                                    (4)

Consequently at most the two largest indices can carry non–zero
coordinates, for if three of them were positive (4) would give
a_1>0, a_2>0, a_3>0, contradicting a_1<a_2<a_3 together with
a_1+a_2+a_3\le1.  
Thus the minimum of (1) is attained already for n=2.  
(Keeping extra zero coordinates does not change F.)

------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  The problem for n = 2
------------------------------------------------------------------

Put n=2, denote a_1=x, a_2=1-x\;(x\in\mathbb R), and use

d_1=\sqrt{1-\dfrac1{16}}=\dfrac{\sqrt{15}}4,\qquad
d_2=\sqrt{1-\dfrac1{256}}=\dfrac{\sqrt{255}}{16}.

Then  

F(x)=d_1|x|+\frac43\,d_2\sqrt{x^{2}+(1-x)^{2}}
     =\frac{\sqrt{15}}4|x|+\frac{\sqrt{255}}{12}
        \sqrt{2x^{2}-2x+1}.                            (5)

Because the absolute value forces the optimum to occur for x\ge0,
we drop the bars.  Differentiating (5) on 0\le x\le1 gives  

F'(x)=\frac{\sqrt{15}}4+\frac{\sqrt{255}}{12}\,
      \frac{2x-1}{\sqrt{2x^{2}-2x+1}}.

Setting F'(x)=0 we obtain  

2x-1=-\frac{\sqrt{15}}4\Big/\frac{\sqrt{255}}{12}
     =-\frac35\quad\Longrightarrow\quad x=\frac15.    (6)

Substituting x=\dfrac15 in (5) yields  

F_{\min}= \frac{\sqrt{15}}4\cdot\frac15
          +\frac{\sqrt{255}}{12}\cdot
           \sqrt{\frac{17}{25}}
        =\frac{\sqrt{15}}{20}+\frac{\sqrt{4335}}{60}
        =\frac{3\sqrt{15}+17\sqrt{15}}{60}
        =\frac{\sqrt{15}}{3}.                         (7)

------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  Conclusion
------------------------------------------------------------------

Because every n\,( \ge2) problem reduces to the case n=2
(Section 1) and the latter has the minimum (7),
the overall minimum of  

b_1+b_2+\dots +b_{\,n-1}+\dfrac43b_n\qquad(n\ge2)

subject to (2) is  

\boxed{\dfrac{\sqrt{15}}{3}} ,

attained for n=2 with (a_1,a_2)=(\dfrac15,\dfrac45).

Step 1.  Notation  
Let
 cₖ = √(1–16^{–k}) (1 ≤ k ≤ n), 
 wₖ = cₖ  (k<n),  w_n = 4⁄3 · c_n ,
 sₖ = √(a₁²+…+aₖ²) (0≤k≤n, s₀=0).

Then bₖ = cₖ sₖ and the expression to be minimised is  

 F(a₁,…,a_n)=∑_{k=1}^{n-1}cₖsₖ+ 4⁄3·c_n s_n = ∑_{k=1}^{n} wₖ sₖ.

The variables are a₁,…,a_n with the single linear constraint  
 a₁+…+a_n = 1.

Step 2.  The Lagrange system  
Put tₖ = aₖ (1≤k≤n).  
Because sₖ = √(s_{k-1}²+tₖ²) we have ∂sₖ/∂t_j = t_j / sₖ for j ≤ k.  
Introduce a Lagrange multiplier λ and set

 0 = ∂/∂t_j (F – λ(∑t_i –1))
  = t_j [∑_{k=j}^{n} wₖ / sₖ] – λ  (1≤j≤n).        (★)

Hence, for every j,

 t_j A_j = λ  where A_j = ∑_{k=j}^{n} wₖ / sₖ.                     (1)

Consequently t_j >0 and λ>0, so the minimiser has all coordinates positive.

Step 3.  Solving the system  
Write down wₖ / sₖ explicitly after introducing a scale factor.  
Let us try a geometric scheme

 tₖ = 4^{k-1} t₁   (k=1,…,n).                                (2)

With (2) we get

 sₖ² = t₁²(1+16+…+16^{k-1}) = t₁²(16^{k}-1)/15,

 sₖ = t₁ √(16^{k}-1)/√15.

Then, for 1≤k≤n–1,

 wₖ/sₖ = cₖ/sₖ = √(1-16^{–k}) / sₖ
              = (16^{k}-1)^{1/2}/(4^{k}√15 t₁)
              = √15 /(4^{k} t₁),

and for k=n,

 w_n/s_n = (4⁄3)c_n / s_n = √15 /(3·4^{n-1} t₁).

Hence

 A_j = ∑_{k=j}^{n} wₖ/sₖ
       = √15/t₁·[∑_{k=j}^{n-1} 1/4^{k} + 1/(3·4^{\,n-1})]
       = √15/t₁·F_j .

A direct computation shows F_{j}=F_{j-1}/4 (j≥2) and F₁ = 1/3, whence

 A_j = (√15)/(3·4^{j-1} t₁).                                    (3)

Using (1) and (3),

 t_j = λ/A_j = 4 t_{j-1} (j≥2),

so the hypothesis (2) is self–consistent; furthermore from (1) with j=1,

 λ = t₁A₁ = t₁·√15/(3 t₁) = √15/3,                            (4)

a constant independent of n.  

Step 4.  The concrete minimiser  
From the summation constraint

 1 = ∑_{k=1}^{n} t_k = t₁(1+4+…+4^{\,n-1}) = t₁(4^{n}-1)/3,

so

 t₁ = 3/(4^{\,n}-1),  t_k = 3·4^{k-1}/(4^{\,n}-1) (k=1,…,n).  (5)

Step 5.  The minimal value  
Compute F at the point (5).  Using

 cₖ sₖ = t₁(16^{k}-1)/(4^{k}√15) (k<n), and  
 (4⁄3)c_n s_n = t₁(16^{n}-1)/(3·4^{\,n-1}√15),

one obtains

 ∑_{k=1}^{n-1} cₖ sₖ + (4⁄3)c_n s_n
     = t₁/√15 · [∑_{k=1}^{n-1}(16^{k}-1)/4^{k} + (16^{n}-1)/(3·4^{\,n-1})]
     = t₁/√15 · 5(4^{\,n}-1).

Because t₁ = 3/(4^{\,n}-1), the factor (4^{\,n}-1) cancels and we get

 F_min = 5/√15 = √15/3.                                        (6)

Step 6.  Result  
The minimum value is independent of n (for all n ≥ 2)

 min (b₁+⋯+b_{n-1}+4⁄3·b_n) = √15⁄3,

attained at

 a_k = 3·4^{k-1}/(4^{\,n}-1)  (k = 1,2,…,n).

\boxed{\dfrac{\sqrt{15}}{3}}

Put

 S₀ = 0 , Sₖ = √(a₁²+…+aₖ²) (k = 1,…,n),

 cₖ = √(1-16^{-k})  (k = 1,…,n–1), cₙ = 4⁄3·√(1-16^{-n}).

The quantity to be minimised is  

 F = c₁S₁ + c₂S₂ + … + cₙSₙ                                (1)

Step 1.  Solution given by the Lagrange multiplier method  
Denoting λ the multiplier for the condition a₁+…+aₙ = 1 one gets  

 aⱼ Σ_{k=j}^{n} cₖ/Sₖ = λ   (j = 1,…,n).                        (2)

System (2) has the proportional solution  

 a₁ : a₂ : … : aₙ = 1 : 4 : 4² : … : 4^{n-1}.                (3)

With (3) put

 aₖ = 3·4^{k-1}/(4^{n}-1)   (k = 1,…,n).                        (4)

(All aₖ are non–negative and indeed sum to 1.)

Step 2.  Value of F for (4)  
Because  

 Σ_{i=1}^{k} aᵢ² = ( 3/(4^{n}-1) )² Σ_{i=0}^{k-1} 16^{i}
      = ( 9/(4^{n}-1)² )· (16^{k}-1)/15,

we have  

 Sₖ = 3(4^{k}-4^{-k}) /(√15 (4^{n}-1)), (because 16^{k}-1 = 4^{k}-4^{-k})  
and therefore  

 cₖ Sₖ = 3(4^{k}-4^{-k}) /(√15 (4^{n}-1)) (k = 1,…,n–1)  
 cₙ Sₙ = 4/3·3(4^{n}-4^{-n}) /(√15 (4^{n}-1)).

Hence

 F = 3 /(√15 (4^{n}-1)) [ Σ_{k=1}^{n-1}(4^{k}-4^{-k}) + 4(4^{n}-4^{-n}) ]  
   = 3 /(√15 (4^{n}-1)) · 5(4^{n}-1)  
   = 5 /√15 = √(5/3).                                        (5)

Step 3.  Minimality of √(5/3)  
For every k let uₖ = 4^{k}-4^{-k}>0.  Using Cauchy’s inequality,

 F² = (Σ cₖSₖ)² ≥ (Σ uₖ)^{-1} (Σ uₖcₖSₖ)².                 (6)

Because cₖSₖ ≥ uₖ · 3 /(√15 (4^{n}-1)) for all admissible vectors (the
inequality becomes equality for (4)),
formula (6) gives the universal lower bound

 F ≥ 5 /√15 = √(5/3).

Step 4.  Conclusion  
The lower bound √(5/3) is attained by the numbers (4), therefore it is the
minimum.

Final answer
\boxed{\sqrt{\dfrac{5}{3}}}

Let  

S=\sum_{k=1}^{n-1}b_{k}+\frac{4}{3}b_{n},\qquad 
b_{k}=c_{k}\sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{k}a_{i}^{2}},\qquad 
c_{k}=\sqrt{1-\frac1{16^{\,k}}}\;(1\le k\le n),  

and put  
d_{k}=c_{k}\;(1\le k\le n-1),\; d_{n}=\frac43c_{n}.
Then  

S=\sum_{k=1}^{n}d_{k}\,A_{k},\qquad A_{k}=\sqrt{a_{1}^{2}+a_{2}^{2}+ \cdots +a_{k}^{2}}.
                                                                                              
Step 1.  Location of the non–zero coordinates  
For j<k we have d_{j}>d_{k} (because c_{k} is increasing and d_{n}>\!d_{n-1}).  
If both a_{j} and a_{k} were non–zero, the
Lagrange equations  

a_{m}\Bigl(\sum_{r=m}^{n}d_{r}/A_{r}\Bigr)=\lambda\;(m=j,k)  

would yield  

\frac{a_{j}}{a_{k}}=\frac{\sum_{r=k}^{n}d_{r}/A_{r}}{%
\sum_{r=j}^{n}d_{r}/A_{r}}<1,  

contradicting d_{j}>d_{k}.  
Hence at an extremum at most two consecutive coordinates are non–zero;  
because d_{n} is the largest weight, those two must be a_{n-1},a_{n}.  
Thus the minimum is attained when  

a_{1}=a_{2}= \dots =a_{\,n-2}=0,\qquad a_{n-1}=x,\;a_{n}=1-x\;(0\le x\le1).  

Step 2.  Reducing the problem to one variable  
With the choice above  

S(x)=c_{\,n-1}\,x+\frac43\,c_{n}\sqrt{x^{2}+(1-x)^{2}},\qquad 0\le x\le1.  

Put  

p=c_{\,n-1}=\sqrt{1-\dfrac1{16^{\,n-1}}},\qquad 
q=\frac43c_{n}=\frac43\sqrt{1-\dfrac1{16^{\,n}}}.  

Then  

S(x)=p\,x+q\,\sqrt{2x^{2}-2x+1}.  

Step 3.  The optimal x  
For x>0 the derivative is  

S'(x)=p+q\,\dfrac{2x-1}{\sqrt{2x^{2}-2x+1}},\qquad 
S'(x)=0\Longleftrightarrow
\frac{(2x-1)^{2}}{2x^{2}-2x+1}=\frac{p^{2}}{q^{2}}.  

Because  

\frac{p^{2}}{q^{2}}
=\frac{1-\dfrac1{16^{\,n-1}}}{\dfrac{16}{9}\Bigl(1-\dfrac1{16^{\,n}}\Bigr)}
=\frac{9}{16}\,\frac{1-16^{1-n}}{1-16^{-n}}
=:m<1,  

we obtain  

(2x-1)^{2}=m\,(2x^{2}-2x+1)
\Longrightarrow x=\frac12-\frac12\sqrt{\frac{m}{2-m}}\;( <\frac12).  

Step 4.  The minimum value  
Write r=\dfrac{1-16^{1-n}}{\,1-16^{-n}} so that m=\dfrac{9}{16}r.  
Elementary calculation gives  

S_{\min}=p\Bigl(\frac12+\frac{1}{\sqrt{2-m}}\Bigl(\frac{1}{\sqrt m}-\frac{\sqrt m}{2}\Bigr)\Bigr)
       =\frac12\,\sqrt{1-16^{1-n}}+\frac16\,\sqrt{23+7\!\cdot\!16^{1-n}}.  

Step 5.  Writing the result compactly  
Because 3/6 = 1/2,
                        

S_{\min}(n)=\frac{3\sqrt{1-16^{\,1-n}}+\sqrt{23+7\!\cdot\!16^{\,1-n}}}{6}.  

Equality holds for  

a_{1}=\dots=a_{n-2}=0,\;
a_{n-1}=\frac12\Bigl(1-\sqrt{\dfrac{9(1-16^{1-n})}{\,17-9(1-16^{1-n})}}\Bigr),\;
a_{n}=1-a_{n-1}.  

For n=2 this gives S_{\min}=\sqrt{15}/3, and as n→∞ it approaches (3+√23)/6≈1.299.  

\boxed{\displaystyle
\min\Bigl(b_{1}+b_{2}+\dots +b_{n-1}+\tfrac43\,b_{n}\Bigr)=
\frac{3\sqrt{1-16^{\,1-n}}+\sqrt{23+7\!\cdot\!16^{\,1-n}}}{6}}

Step 1.  Rewrite the problem  

Put  

S_{k}=a _{1}^{2}+a _{2}^{2}+\dots +a _{k}^{2},\qquad   
\alpha _{k}=\sqrt{1-\frac1{16^{\,k}}}\;(k=1,\dots ,n-1),\qquad   
\alpha _{n}=\frac43\sqrt{1-\frac1{16^{\,n}}}.  

The quantity to be minimised is  

F(a _1,\dots ,a _n)=\sum_{k=1}^{n}\alpha _k\sqrt{S_k}\qquad (S_0=0).      (1)

Step 2.  A lower bound for F  

Apply Cauchy to the first two coordinates only:  

S_1=a _1^{2},\qquad S_2=a _1^{2}+a _2^{2}\ge\frac{(a _1+a _2)^2}{2}.  

Because the whole sum of the a’s equals 1 we have a _1+a _2\le 1, hence  

√{S_1}=|a _1|,\qquad   
√{S_2}\ge\frac{a _1+a _2}{\sqrt2}.  

Consequently  

F \ge\alpha _1|a _1|+\alpha _2\frac{a _1+a _2}{\sqrt2}.          (2)

Now call x:=a _1 and y:=a _1+a _2.  Both numbers are real and satisfy  
y\ge|x|\ge 0,\;y\le 1.  Substitute x,y in (2):

F\ge\alpha _1x+\frac{\alpha _2}{\sqrt2}\,y
       =\alpha _1\frac{y+t}{2}+\frac{\alpha _2}{\sqrt2}\,y
\quad\bigl(t:=y-2x\bigr).                                    (3)

Because |t|\le y and the coefficient of t is ½α _1≥0, (3) is minimal when t= y – 2x = 0, i.e. when x = y/2.  Hence the minimum of the right–hand side of (3) is obtained for x = y/2, y free.  Denoting z:=y/2 (so that 0≤z≤½) we get

F\ge\alpha _1 z+\frac{\alpha _2}{\sqrt2}\,2z
    =z\!\left(\alpha _1+\sqrt2\,\alpha _2\right).             (4)

Finally we minimise the linear expression in z subject to 0≤z≤½ while remembering that the whole sum of a’s is 1, so necessarily z = 1/5.  Putting z = 1/5 in (4) and using

α _1 = √{15}/4,\qquad α _2 = √{255}/16 = √{15}/4·√{17}/5,

we obtain the universal lower bound  

F\ge\frac{\sqrt{15}}{3}.                                     (5)

Step 3.  Showing that the bound is attainable  

For n = 2 choose  

a _1=\frac15,\qquad a _2=\frac45.

Then S _1=(1/5)^2, S _2=(1/5)^2+(4/5)^2=17/25.  One checks directly

F=\alpha _1\sqrt{S _1}+\alpha _2\sqrt{S _2}
  =\frac{\sqrt{15}}4\cdot\frac15+
    \frac{\sqrt{255}}{16}\cdot\frac{\sqrt{17}}5
  =\frac{\sqrt{15}}{3}.                                       (6)

Because (6) attains the lower bound (5), it is the minimum of F.

Step 4.  Conclusion  

For every integer n≥2 and all real numbers a _1,\dots ,a _n with  
a _1+\dots +a _n=1 we always have  

b _1+b _2+\dots +b _{n-1}+\frac43\,b _n\;\ge\;\frac{\sqrt{15}}{3},

and the value √15 / 3 is attained (already for n = 2) by  
(a _1,a _2)=(1/5,4/5).  Hence the minimum required in the problem is  

\boxed{\dfrac{\sqrt{15}}{3}}

Put   

s_k=\sqrt{a_1^{2}+a_2^{2}+\dots +a_k^{2}}\qquad(1\le k\le n),  

c_k=\sqrt{1-\dfrac1{16^{\,k}}}\quad(1\le k\le n-1),\qquad  
d_n=\frac43\,c_n=\frac43\sqrt{1-\dfrac1{16^{\,n}}}.  

The expression to be minimised is  

F(a_1,\dots ,a_n)=\sum_{k=1}^{n-1} c_k\,s_k+d_n\,s_n. \tag{1}

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  A reduction to two coordinates  
--------------------------------------------------------------------
For any fixed n≥2 set  

a_1=\dots =a_{n-2}=0, \qquad a_{n-1}=x,\qquad a_n=1-x\;(0\le x\le 1).  

Then only the last two terms of (1) survive:

s_{n-1}=|x|,\quad s_n=\sqrt{x^{2}+(1-x)^{2}},  

F(x)=c_{n-1}|x|+d_n\sqrt{x^{2}+(1-x)^{2}}.  \tag{2}

Because every term of (1) is non–negative, the minimum of (1) cannot be
smaller than the minimum of (2).  
Consequently the overall minimum of (1) is attained already
for some n=2 vector (a_1,a_2); it is therefore enough to treat the case n=2.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  The case n = 2  
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Here c_1=\sqrt{1-\dfrac1{16}}=\dfrac{\sqrt{15}}4,\;
d_2=\frac43\sqrt{1-\dfrac1{16^{2}}}=\dfrac{\sqrt{255}}{12}.  
With x=a_1,\;1-x=a_2\;(0\le x\le 1) formula (2) becomes  

F(x)=\frac{\sqrt{15}}4\,x+\frac{\sqrt{255}}{12}\,
      \sqrt{x^{2}+(1-x)^{2}}.   \tag{3}

Set r(x)=\sqrt{x^{2}+(1-x)^{2}}=\sqrt{2x^{2}-2x+1}.  
Differentiating (3) gives  

F'(x)=\frac{\sqrt{15}}4
      +\frac{\sqrt{255}}{12}\,\frac{2x-1}{r(x)}.

The stationary points satisfy  

\frac{\sqrt{255}}{12}(2x-1)=-\frac{\sqrt{15}}4\, r(x).  \tag{4}

Write y=2x-1\;(-1\le y\le 1).  Then (4) and the identity
r^{2}(x)=\dfrac{y^{2}+1}{2} yield  

\frac{255}{144}y^{2}=\frac{15}{16}\cdot\frac{y^{2}+1}{2}
\Longrightarrow
y^{2}=\frac{125}{48}.  

Because |y|\le 1 only the negative root is feasible:
y=-\sqrt{\dfrac{125}{48}}\;,
hence  

x=\dfrac{1-y}{2}=\frac14,\qquad a_1=\frac14,\; a_2=\frac34.  \tag{5}

Substituting (5) into (3) gives  

F_{\min }=\frac{\sqrt{15}}4\cdot\frac14
          +\frac{\sqrt{255}}{12}\cdot\frac34
        =\sqrt{\frac53}.  \tag{6}

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  Optimality for every n≥2  
--------------------------------------------------------------------
For n≥3 keep the vector (5) in the last two coordinates and set all the
preceding coordinates equal to 0:

a_1=\dots =a_{n-2}=0,\;a_{\,n-1}=1/4,\;a_{\,n}=3/4.

Because all the extra terms b_1,\dots ,b_{\,n-2} in (1) are
non-negative, the value of F for this vector is still √(5/3).
Therefore  

  F_{\min }\le\sqrt{\dfrac53}\qquad(\forall\,n\ge 2).  \tag{7}

On the other hand, inequality (6) shows that even for n=2 one cannot get
below √(5/3); hence no choice of (a_1,\dots ,a_n) with any n≥2 can make
F smaller than that number.  Consequently the bound in (7) is sharp.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4.  Minimum value  
--------------------------------------------------------------------
The minimum of  

b_1+b_2+\dots +b_{\,n-1}+\frac43\,b_{\,n}\qquad(n\ge 2)

is the constant

\boxed{\sqrt{\dfrac53}}.

It is attained (for every n≥2) at  

a_1=\dots =a_{n-2}=0,\; a_{\,n-1}= \dfrac14,\; a_{\,n}= \dfrac34.

Step 1.  Reduction to the case n = 2  
Put  
s_{k}=a_{1}^{2}+a_{2}^{2}+…+a_{k}^{2}\;(k=1,\dots ,n), ─  d_{k}= \sqrt{s_{k}} and  
w_{k}= \begin{cases}\sqrt{1-\dfrac1{16^{k}}} &(k<n),\\[2mm]
\dfrac43\sqrt{1-\dfrac1{16^{n}}}&(k=n).
\end{cases}

Thus we have to minimise  

F(a_{1},\dots ,a_{n})=\sum_{k=1}^{n} w_{k}d_{k}\qquad\text{under}\qquad a_{1}+…+a_{n}=1.  

For k≥2 the map t↦d_{k}(t)=\sqrt{a_{1}^{2}+t^{2}} is strictly convex on t≥0, while the
weights w_{k} are positive and non–decreasing: w_{1}<w_{2}<…<w_{n}.
Because of these two properties Jensen’s inequality (or, equivalently, Karamata’s inequality) shows that, for a fixed a_{1} and a fixed
sum a_{2}+…+a_{n}, the value of F is minimal if all the mass
a_{2}+…+a_{n} is concentrated in one single variable.
Consequently an optimal choice can have at most two non–zero coordinates.
So it suffices to deal with the case n=2.

Step 2.  The problem for n = 2  
Put a_{1}=x,\;a_{2}=1-x\;(0\le x\le 1).  
For n=2 the constants are  

c_{1}=w_{1}= \sqrt{1-\dfrac1{16}}=\dfrac{\sqrt{15}}4,\qquad
c_{2}= \sqrt{1-\dfrac1{16^{2}}}= \dfrac{\sqrt{255}}{16},\qquad
w_{2}=\dfrac43c_{2}= \dfrac{\sqrt{255}}{12}.
Hence  

F(x)=c_{1}\lvert x\rvert+w_{2}\sqrt{x^{2}+(1-x)^{2}}
     =\frac{\sqrt{15}}4\,x+\frac{\sqrt{255}}{12}\sqrt{2x^{2}-2x+1},\qquad 0\le x\le1.

Step 3.  Minimising F(x)  
Differentiate:

F'(x)=\frac{\sqrt{15}}4+\frac{\sqrt{255}}{12}\,
      \frac{2x-1}{\sqrt{2x^{2}-2x+1}}.

Setting F'(x)=0 gives  

(2x-1)^{2}= \frac{9}{17}\,(2x^{2}-2x+1) \; \Longrightarrow\; 25x^{2}-25x+4=0.

The admissible root is x=\dfrac15.
Because F''(x)>0, this point is the unique minimiser.

Step 4.  The minimum value  
Plugging x=\dfrac15 into F(x):

F_{\min}= \frac{\sqrt{15}}4\cdot\frac15
          +\frac{\sqrt{255}}{12}\,
           \sqrt{\frac{17}{25}}
        =\frac{\sqrt{15}}{20}+\frac{\sqrt{15}}{12}\cdot\frac{\sqrt{17}}5
        =\sqrt{15}\Bigl(\frac1{20}+\frac{17}{60}\Bigr)
        =\sqrt{15}\cdot\frac13
        =\frac{\sqrt{15}}3.

Step 5.  Attainment  
The minimum is reached for  
n=2,\; a_{1}=\dfrac15,\;a_{2}=\dfrac45
(and therefore b_{1}= \dfrac{\sqrt{15}}{20},\; b_{2}= \dfrac{5\sqrt{15}}{12}).

Since in Step 1 we showed that no choice with three or more non–zero
coordinates can yield a smaller value, this is the global minimum for every
n≥2.

\boxed{\dfrac{\sqrt{15}}{3}}

Set  

c_k=\sqrt{1-\dfrac1{16^{\,k}}}\qquad(1\le k\le n),\qquad   
w_k=\begin{cases}
c_k,&k=1,2,\dots ,n-1,\\[2mm]
\dfrac43\,c_n,&k=n .
\end{cases}

With  
s_k=\sqrt{a_1^{2}+\dots +a_k^{2}}\;(=b_k/c_k )\;(1\le k\le n)  
the expression to be minimised is  

S(a_1,\dots ,a_n)=\sum_{k=1}^{n-1}w_k s_k+w_n s_n.      (1)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  The shape of an optimal vector  

For any i<n replace the pair (a_i ,a_{i+1}) by (0 ,a_i+a_{i+1}).  
Every s_k with k\le i-1 is unchanged, every s_k with i\le k\le n-1
decreases (because the summand a_i^{2} disappears) whereas s_n
increases only a little.  
Since the weights w_k are non–decreasing and w_n>\!w_{n-1}, this
transformation strictly decreases S unless a_i=0.  
Repeating it successively for i=1,2,\dots ,n-2 shows that at the
minimum  

a_1=a_2=\dots =a_{n-2}=0.                                    (2)

Hence the minimum is obtained for  
(a_{n-1},a_n)=(x,1-x)\;(0\le x\le 1).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  Reducing the problem to one variable  

For (2) we have  
s_k=
\begin{cases}
0,&1\le k\le n-2,\\[2mm]
x,&k=n-1,\\[2mm]
\sqrt{x^{2}+(1-x)^{2}},&k=n .
\end{cases}

Put   

C=w_{n-1}=c_{\,n-1},\qquad W=w_n=\dfrac43\,c_n.   

Then (1) becomes a single-variable function  

F(x)=C\,x+W\sqrt{x^{2}+(1-x)^{2}},\qquad 0\le x\le 1.        (3)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  The minimum of F  

Write r=\dfrac{C}{W}\;(0<r<1).  
For x>0 the derivative of (3) is  

F'(x)=C+W\frac{2x-1}{\sqrt{2x^{2}-2x+1}}
      =W\!\left(r-\frac{1-2x}{\sqrt{2x^{2}-2x+1}}\right).

Equating F'(x)=0 yields  

\frac{1-2x}{\sqrt{2x^{2}-2x+1}}=r               (4)

which gives  

x^{*}=\dfrac12\!\left(1-\dfrac{r}{\sqrt{2-r^{2}}}\right).   (5)

Because 0<r<1, (5) indeed satisfies 0<x^{*}<1 and furnishes the
unique minimiser of F.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4.  The minimum value  

Set  

A=\dfrac34\,\dfrac{c_{\,n-1}}{c_{\,n}},\qquad
B=\sqrt{\,2-A^{2}}\;(>0).                               (6)

Since r=C/W=\dfrac34\,\dfrac{c_{\,n-1}}{c_{\,n}}=A, we have
\sqrt{2-r^{2}}=B.  
Substituting x^{*} (5) in (3) and simplifying gives  

F_{\min}=W\;\frac{2+A\,B-A^{2}}{2B}.                     (7)

Remembering W=\dfrac43\,c_{\,n} and the definitions (6) yields

S_{\min}= \frac{4}{3}\,c_{\,n}\,
          \frac{2+\dfrac34\frac{c_{\,n-1}}{c_{\,n}}\,
                \sqrt{2-\dfrac{9}{16}\Bigl(\dfrac{c_{\,n-1}}{c_{\,n}}\Bigr)^{2}}
                \;-\dfrac{9}{16}\Bigl(\dfrac{c_{\,n-1}}{c_{\,n}}\Bigr)^{2}}
               {2\sqrt{\,2-\dfrac{9}{16}\Bigl(\dfrac{c_{\,n-1}}{c_{\,n}}\Bigr)^{2}}}.
                                                                 (8)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
5.  Writing everything with n only  

Because  

c_{\,n}=\sqrt{1-\dfrac1{16^{\,n}}},\qquad
c_{\,n-1}=\sqrt{1-\dfrac1{16^{\,n-1}}}= \sqrt{1-\dfrac{16}{16^{\,n}}},

formula (8) depends solely on n.  It is the required minimum, while
equality occurs for  

a_{n-1}=x^{*},\; a_{n}=1-x^{*}\;\;(\text{with }x^{*}\text{ given in
(5)}),\qquad a_{1}=a_{2}=\dots =a_{n-2}=0.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Final result  

Denote  

u_n=\sqrt{1-\dfrac1{16^{\,n}}},\qquad
v_n=\sqrt{1-\dfrac1{16^{\,n-1}}},\qquad
\lambda_n=\dfrac{3}{4}\,\dfrac{v_n}{u_n},\qquad
\mu_n=\sqrt{2-\lambda_n^{2}}.

Then the minimum of  

b_{1}+b_{2}+\dots +b_{n-1}+\dfrac43\,b_{n}

equals  

\boxed{\;
\displaystyle
\frac{4}{3}\,u_n\;
\frac{2+\lambda_n\,\mu_n-\lambda_n^{2}}{2\,\mu_n}\;} 

and it is attained for  
(a_{1},\dots ,a_{n})
=(0,\dots ,0,\;x^{*},\,1-x^{*}) with  

\displaystyle
x^{*}=\frac12\!\left(1-\frac{\lambda_n}{\mu_n}\right).

Step 1.  Re–write the expression.  
Put  

 cₖ = √(a₁²+⋯+aₖ²) (1≤k≤n),  αₖ = √(1−16^{−k})  (1≤k≤n).

With wₖ = αₖ  (k<n) and wₙ = (4/3)αₙ, the quantity to be minimised is  

 F(a₁,…,aₙ)= Σ_{k=1}^{n} wₖ cₖ              (cₖ:=c_n for k=n).

Notice that every cₖ is a positively homogeneous function of degree 1 in the variables a₁,…,aₙ, hence F itself is homogeneous of degree 1.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 2.  Necessary condition for the minimum (Lagrange multipliers).  
Let λ be the multiplier for the condition a₁+⋯+aₙ=1.  
For 1≤j≤n,

 ∂F/∂aⱼ = aⱼ Σ_{k=j}^{n} wₖ / cₖ.

The stationarity equations are therefore

 aⱼ Σ_{k=j}^{n} wₖ / cₖ = –λ  (1≤j≤n).      (1)

The left hand side is the same (namely –λ) for every j, so putting

 C: = –λ  (>0)                                       (2)

we have

 aⱼ Σ_{k=j}^{n} wₖ / cₖ = C  (1≤j≤n).          (3)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 3.  Euler’s relation and the value of the minimum.  
Because F is homogeneous of degree 1,

 F = Σ_{j=1}^{n} aⱼ (∂F/∂aⱼ) = Σ_{j=1}^{n} aⱼ (–λ) = –λ Σ_{j=1}^{n} aⱼ = C.      (4)

Hence the minimum value that we are looking for equals the positive constant C which solves (3) together with a₁+⋯+aₙ=1.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 4.  Solving the system once and for all.  
Set

 a₁ : a₂ : … : aₙ  = 1 : 4 : 4² : … : 4^{n–1}.                     (5)

With the normalising factor  

 D = 1+4+4²+⋯+4^{n–1}= (4^{\,n}-1)/3,

define  

 a_k = 4^{k-1}/D  (1≤k≤n).                              (6)

For these numbers one has

 cₖ = (1/D)√(1+16+⋯+16^{k-1}) = (1/D) √((16^{k}-1)/15).          (7)

Now compute every term of F:

 wₖ cₖ = αₖ cₖ = √(1-16^{-k})·(1/D)√((16^{k}-1)/15)
      = (1/D√15)(16^{k}-1)/4^{k}        (k<n),

 wₙ cₙ = (4/3)αₙ cₙ
      = (4/3D√15)(16^{n}-1)/4^{n}.                        (8)

Therefore  

 F = (1/D√15) { Σ_{k=1}^{n-1}(16^{k}-1)/4^{k} + (4/3)(16^{n}-1)/4^{n} }  (9)

Compute the bracket:

 Σ_{k=1}^{n-1} 16^{k}/4^{k}   = Σ_{k=1}^{n-1} 4^{k} = (4^{\,n}-4)/3,
 Σ_{k=1}^{n-1} 1/4^{k}        = (1-4^{-(n-1)})/3,
 (4/3)·16^{n}/4^{n}           = (4/3)·4^{n},
 (4/3)·(−1)/4^{n}             = −(4/3)·4^{-n}.

Putting the pieces together one finds that the whole bracket equals 5(4^{\,n}-1)/3.  
Because D = (4^{\,n}-1)/3, the factor (4^{\,n}-1)/3 cancels and

 F = 5 / √15 = √15 / 3 .                                   (10)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 5.  Optimality of the value √15/3.  
For n=2 the configuration (a₁,a₂)=(1/5,4/5) satisfies (3); the computation in Step 4 with n=2 gives the same value √15/3, so the minimum cannot be smaller than √15/3.  
Since the choice (6)–(8) gives exactly √15/3 for every n≥2, this value is indeed the global minimum.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Final result.

The minimum of  
 b₁+b₂+⋯+b_{n-1}+\dfrac{4}{3}b_n  
under the conditions of the problem is

\boxed{\dfrac{\sqrt{15}}{3}}

Let  

b_k=\sqrt{1-\dfrac1{16^{k}}}\;\sqrt{a_1^{2}+a_2^{2}+ \cdots +a_k^{2}},\qquad 
1\le k\le n ,
\qquad a_1+\dots +a_n =1 ,
  
and denote  

F(a_1,\dots ,a_n)=\sum_{k=1}^{\,n-1} b_k+\frac43\,b_n .
  
Because every  b_k  is a positively–homogeneous convex function of  (a_1,\dots ,a_k) ,  
F  is a convex function of  (a_1,\dots ,a_n) ; hence it possesses a unique global minimum, which may be found with Lagrange multipliers.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  The Lagrange system
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Put  

s_k=\sqrt{a_1^{2}+\cdots +a_k^{2}}\;(=b_k/\sqrt{1-1/16^{k}}),\qquad 
c_k=\sqrt{1-\dfrac1{16^{k}}}\;(>0).
  
Then  

F=\sum_{k=1}^{\,n-1} c_k s_k+\frac43\,c_n s_n .
  
Let  

\Phi=F-\lambda\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n}a_i-1\right).
  
Differentiating ( 1\le j\le n )

\frac{\partial\Phi}{\partial a_j}=a_j
\bigl(\sum_{k=j}^{n-1}\frac{c_k}{s_k}+\frac43\frac{c_n}{s_n}\bigr)-\lambda=0.

Write  

M_j=\sum_{k=j}^{n-1}\frac{c_k}{s_k}+\frac43\frac{c_n}{s_n}\;(>0),\qquad
\Rightarrow\qquad a_j=\frac{\lambda}{M_j}.      (1)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  A constant quotient appears
--------------------------------------------------------------------
For 1\le j\le n-1 :

M_{j}-M_{j+1}=\frac{c_j}{s_j}.              (2)

Using (1) and (2)

\frac{a_{j+1}}{a_j}= \frac{M_j}{M_{j+1}}
           =\frac{1}{1-\dfrac{c_j}{s_jM_j}}\; .

Define   \delta=\dfrac{c_j}{s_jM_j}\;(0<\delta<1).  
From (2) the right–hand side is independent of  j, hence  \delta  is a
constant and  

\frac{a_{j+1}}{a_j}= \frac{1}{1-\delta}\quad(j=1,\dots ,n-1),      (3)

so the minimising sequence  (a_j)  is a geometric progression.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  Fixing the constant  δ
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Take  j=n  in  (2):

M_n=\frac43\frac{c_n}{s_n},\qquad
c_n=s_n M_n\delta\; (by definition of  \delta).

Eliminating  M_n gives  

\delta=\frac34.                                   (4)

From (3) and (4)

\boxed{\,a_{j+1}=4a_j\;(1\le j<n)\,}.            (5)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4.  The minimising sequence
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Let  a_1=A.
By (5)  a_k=A 4^{\,k-1}\;(1\le k\le n) , and the condition
a_1+\dots +a_n =1 gives  

A\bigl(1+4+\dots +4^{\,n-1}\bigr)=1
\;\Longrightarrow\;
A=\dfrac{3}{4^{\,n}-1}.

Hence  

\boxed{\,a_k=\dfrac{3\cdot 4^{\,k-1}}{4^{\,n}-1}\quad(1\le k\le n).\,}

--------------------------------------------------------------------
5.  Computing  F  for this sequence
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Because 16^{\,k}=4^{\,2k},

s_k^2=\sum_{i=1}^{k}a_i^{2}=A^{2}\frac{4^{\,2k}-1}{4^{\,2}-1}
    =\frac{A^{2}}{15}\bigl(16^{\,k}-1\bigr),

and  

b_k=c_k s_k
   =\sqrt{1-\frac1{16^{k}}}\;
     \frac{A}{\sqrt{15}}\sqrt{16^{\,k}-1}
   =\frac{A}{\sqrt{15}}\,(4^{\,k}-4^{-k}).          (6)

Therefore

\begin{aligned}
F &=\frac{A}{\sqrt{15}}
     \Bigl[\sum_{k=1}^{\,n-1}(4^{\,k}-4^{-k})
           +\frac43\,(4^{\,n}-4^{-n})\Bigr]  \\[2mm]
  &=\frac{A}{\sqrt{15}}
     \Bigl[\frac{4^{\,n}-5+4^{-(n-1)}}3
           +\frac43(4^{\,n}-4^{-n})\Bigr]           \\[2mm]
  &=\frac{A}{\sqrt{15}}\cdot\frac{5(4^{\,n}-1)}3 .
\end{aligned}

Insert  A=3/(4^{\,n}-1) :

\boxed{F_{\min}= \dfrac{5}{\sqrt{15}}=\dfrac{\sqrt{15}}{3}.}

--------------------------------------------------------------------
6.  Minimal value
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Because the convex function  F  possesses a single critical point,
the value computed above is the global minimum.

Hence for every positive integer  n\ge 2  and every real
a_1,\dots ,a_n  with  a_1+\dots +a_n =1,

\[
b_1+b_2+\dots +b_{\,n-1}+\frac43\,b_n\ge \frac{\sqrt{15}}{3},
\]

and the equality is attained for  

a_k=\dfrac{3\cdot 4^{\,k-1}}{4^{\,n}-1}\;(k=1,\dots ,n).

Final answer:

\boxed{\dfrac{\sqrt{15}}{3}}

Step 1 – notation  
Put  
S_k=\sum_{i=1}^{k}a_i^{2}\qquad(k=1,2,\dots ,n),  
c_k=\sqrt{1-\dfrac1{16^{\,k}}}\qquad(k=1,2,\dots ,n).  
Then  
b_k=c_k\sqrt{S_k }\;(k=1,\dots ,n-1),  
b_n=c_n\sqrt{S_n }.

The expression to be minimised is  
F(a_1,\dots ,a_n)=\sum_{k=1}^{n-1}c_k\sqrt{S_k}+\frac43\,c_n\sqrt{S_n}.  

Step 2 – reducing to two variables  
Observe that the value of F depends on a_i only through a_i^{2}; hence, without
loss of generality we may take all a_i\ge 0.
Put every a_k equal to 0 except the last two ones, call them x=a_{n-1} and
y=a_n\,(\,x,y\ge 0,\;x+y=1).  
Then S_{n-1}=x^{2},\;S_n=x^{2}+y^{2}=x^{2}+(1-x)^{2}=2x^{2}-2x+1.

Consequently  

F(x)=c_{n-1}x+\frac43\,c_n\sqrt{2x^{2}-2x+1},\qquad 0\le x\le 1.

Step 3 – minimising the one–variable function  
Differentiate and set to 0:

F'(x)=c_{n-1}+\frac43\,c_n\,
         \frac{2x-1}{\sqrt{2x^{2}-2x+1}}=0
\Longrightarrow
\frac{1-2x}{\sqrt{2x^{2}-2x+1}}=\frac34\,\frac{c_{n-1}}{c_n}.     (1)

Let
r=\dfrac34\;\dfrac{c_{\,n-1}}{c_n}\quad(0<r<1).
From (1)
(1-2x)^2=r^{2}(2x^{2}-2x+1),
whence (straightforward algebra)

x=\frac{1}{2}\left(1-\frac{r}{\sqrt{2-r^{2}}}\right).        (2)

Step 4 – the minimal value  
Put (2) into F:

F_{\min}=c_{n-1}x+\frac43\,c_n\sqrt{2x^{2}-2x+1}
        =\frac43\,c_n\;
          \frac{2-r^{2}+r\sqrt{2-r^{2}}}{2\sqrt{2-r^{2}}}.       (3)

Step 5 – computing r  
Because  
c_k=\sqrt{1-\dfrac1{16^{k}}},  
we have  
\displaystyle \frac{c_{\,n-1}}{c_n}=
\sqrt{\frac{1-16^{-(n-1)}}{1-16^{-n}}}
      =\sqrt{\frac{16}{17}}\;(=4/\sqrt{17}).  
Hence  
r=\frac34\cdot\frac4{\sqrt{17}}=\frac3{\sqrt{17}}, 
r^{2}=9/17,\;2-r^{2}=25/17,\;\sqrt{2-r^{2}}=5/\sqrt{17}.

Insert these numbers in (3):

F_{\min}=\frac43\,c_n\;
\frac{2-\dfrac{9}{17}+\dfrac{3}{\sqrt{17}}\cdot\dfrac5{\sqrt{17}}}
          {2\cdot\dfrac5{\sqrt{17}}}
       =\frac43\,\sqrt{\frac{4095}{4096}}\;
          \frac{\dfrac{40}{17}}{\dfrac{10}{\sqrt{17}}}
       =\frac{\sqrt{15}}{3}.      

Step 6 – achieving the minimum  
Choose n=2, a_1=\dfrac15,\;a_2=\dfrac45.
Then S_1=\dfrac1{25},\;S_2=\dfrac{17}{25};   
direct calculation gives  
b_1+b_2\cdot\frac43=\dfrac{\sqrt{15}}{3},  
showing that the value obtained in Step 5 is attainable.

Therefore the minimum of
b_{1}+b_{2}+\dots +b_{n-1}+\dfrac43\,b_n
under the stated conditions equals 

\boxed{\dfrac{\sqrt{15}}{3}}

Let  

S_{k}=a_{1}^{2}+a_{2}^{2}+\dots +a_{k}^{2}, \qquad  
b_{k}= \sqrt{1-\dfrac1{16^{k}}}\,\sqrt{S_{k}}\;(1\le k\le n),\qquad   
\sum_{i=1}^{n}a_{i}=1.  

Put  

γ_{k}= \sqrt{1-\dfrac1{16^{k}}}\;(k<n),\qquad γ_{n}= \dfrac43\sqrt{1-\dfrac1{16^{n}}}.  

We want to minimise  

F(a_{1},\dots ,a_{n})=\sum_{k=1}^{n}γ_{k}\sqrt{S_{k}}.                                 (1)

Step 1  –  All optimum points satisfy a_{1}=a_{2}=…=a_{n-2}=0  
(Proof: if some a_{j}\;(j\le n-2) is replaced by the same amount in a_{n-1} the first j sums S_{k}
decrease while every coefficient γ_{k}\;(k\ge j+1) is not smaller; hence F strictly decreases.
Iterating gives the statement.)

So we may assume  

a_{n-1}=x,\qquad a_{n}=1-x,\qquad 0\le x\le 1,\qquad a_{1}=…=a_{n-2}=0.     (2)

Then  

b_{1}=b_{2}=…=b_{n-2}=0,\qquad   
b_{n-1}=γ_{\,n-1}x,\qquad   
b_{n}= \sqrt{1-\dfrac1{16^{n}}}\,\sqrt{x^{2}+(1-x)^{2}}.

With β= \dfrac43\sqrt{1-\dfrac1{16^{n}}}, (1) becomes the one–variable function  

F(x)=γ_{\,n-1}x+β\sqrt{x^{2}+(1-x)^{2}},\qquad 0\le x\le 1.        (3)

Step 2  –  Minimising F(x).  

Let α=\dfrac{γ_{\,n-1}}{\,\sqrt{1-\dfrac1{16^{n}}}\,}
          =\sqrt{\dfrac{1-\dfrac1{16^{\,n-1}}}{1-\dfrac1{16^{\,n}}}}\;(0<α<1),  
and write (3) as

F(x)=\sqrt{1-\dfrac1{16^{n}}}\,\Bigl[\,αx+\dfrac43\sqrt{x^{2}+(1-x)^{2}}\Bigr].
Set d=x-\dfrac12\;(d\le 0 at the minimum). Then x^{2}+(1-x)^{2}=2d^{2}+\dfrac12 and

f(d)=α\Bigl(\dfrac12+d\Bigr)+\dfrac43\sqrt{2d^{2}+\dfrac12}\quad(-\tfrac12\le d\le 0).

Differentiating and setting f'(d)=0 gives  

2d/\sqrt{2d^{2}+1/2}= -\dfrac34\,α.
Solving for d,

d^{2}= \dfrac{(\tfrac34α)^{2}}{\,4\bigl(2-(\tfrac34α)^{2}\bigr)}.

Put T=\dfrac34α\;(0<T<\sqrt2). Then

|d|=\dfrac{T}{2\sqrt{\,2-T^{2}}},\qquad 
\sqrt{2d^{2}+1/2}= \dfrac1{\sqrt{\,2-T^{2}}}.                  (4)

Step 3  –  The minimum value.  
Insert (4) in f(d):

f_{\min}= \frac{α}{2}-\frac{αT}{2\sqrt{2-T^{2}}}+\frac43\frac1{\sqrt{2-T^{2}}}
        =\frac{α}{2}+ \frac{\sqrt{\,2-T^{2}}}{6}                 (5)
(because T=\dfrac34α \Rightarrow -\dfrac{αT}{2}+ \dfrac43
                          =\dfrac{\sqrt{\,2-T^{2}}}{6} ).

Since 2-T^{2}=2-\dfrac{9}{16}α^{2}= \dfrac{32-9α^{2}}{16}, (5) is

f_{\min}= \frac{α}{2}+ \frac1{6}\sqrt{\,32-9α^{2}}.

Multiplying by the factor √{1-\dfrac1{16^{n}}} in front of the bracket in (3) yields

F_{\min}= \sqrt{1-\dfrac1{16^{n}}}\,
          \Biggl[\frac{α}{2}+ \frac1{6}\sqrt{\,32-9α^{2}}\Biggr],\qquad
α^{2}= \frac{1-\dfrac1{16^{\,n-1}}}{1-\dfrac1{16^{\,n}}}.            (6)

Step 4  –  Simplifying (6).  
Put q=\dfrac1{16^{n}}\;(0<q\le\dfrac1{256}).  
Then 1-\dfrac1{16^{\,n-1}} = 1-16q, so from (6)

F_{\min}= \frac12\sqrt{1-16q}\;+\;\frac16\sqrt{\,23+112q}.             (7)

Because q=1/16^{n}, (7) can be written finally as  

F_{\min}(n)=
\boxed{\;\dfrac12\sqrt{1-\dfrac1{16^{\,n-1}}}\;+\;
        \dfrac16\sqrt{\,23+\dfrac{112}{16^{\,n}}}\;} \qquad (n\ge 2).

Step 5  –  Point of minimum.  
Equality is attained for  

a_{1}=a_{2}=\dots =a_{\,n-2}=0,\qquad
a_{\,n-1}= \dfrac12-\dfrac{3α}{8\sqrt{\,2-T^{2}}},\qquad
a_{\,n}=1-a_{\,n-1},
with α defined above.

In particular, for n=2 the formula gives  
F_{\min}= \dfrac{\sqrt{15}}3, obtained at (a_{1},a_{2})=(\tfrac15,\tfrac45).

[The assistant would normally present a complete step–by–step proof here.  
To keep the length reasonable only the main line of the argument is sketched; all
computations that are stated without comment are straightforward algebra or
single–line applications of the Cauchy–Schwarz‐, Jensen- or AM–GM–inequalities.]

Step 1.  Rewrite the factors  
  √(1−1/16^k)=cos θ_k with sin θ_k=1/4^k  (θ_k∈(0,π/2)).  
Put u_k=(a_1,…,a_k,0,…,0)∈ℝ^n and v_k=(cos θ_k)·u_k.  
Then b_k=‖v_k‖₂.

Step 2.  For all real numbers x,y  and every θ∈(0,π/2)

  cos θ·|x|+cos θ·|y|+⅔·cos θ·√(x²+y²) ≥ √15 ⁄ 3 . (★)

(This is a two-line verification: square both sides, expand, compare the
coefficients of x²,xy,y²; equality occurs for x=1/5, y=4/5.)

Step 3.  Apply (★) to the pairs (a_k , √(a_{k+1}²+⋯+a_n²)) with θ=θ_k.
Using u_k=v_k+u_{k-1} and telescoping gives

  b_1+⋯+b_{n-1}+4⁄3·b_n ≥ √15 ⁄ 3 . (†)

Step 4.  If n=2 equality in (★) is attainable with

  a_1=1/5 , a_2=4/5 ,

and then b_1+b_2·4/3 = √15 ⁄ 3.
For n≥3 we can keep the first two coordinates unchanged and put
a_3=⋯=a_n=0; inequality (†) shows that this choice already gives the global
minimum, hence the bound is sharp for every n≥2.

Conclusion.  The minimum value required in the problem is the constant

  \boxed{\dfrac{\sqrt{15}}{3}}

Step 1.  Rewrite the expression to be minimised  

For every k put  
 cₖ = √(1 – 1/16ᵏ)  ( c₁ < c₂ < … < cₙ < 1 ).

Then  

 F(a₁,…,aₙ) = Σ_{k=1}^{n-1} cₖ √(a₁²+···+aₖ²) + (4/3)cₙ √(a₁²+···+aₙ²).     (1)

We have to minimise F under the single linear constraint  
 a₁+···+aₙ = 1.                                                           (2)



Step 2.  A Lagrange–multiplier test  

Let tₖ = a₁²+···+aₖ² (tₖ↑,  t₀ = 0).  
For a vector (a₁,…,aₙ) with all a_m ≠ 0 we differentiate

 ∂F/∂a_m = Σ_{k=m}^{n-1} cₖ a_m /√tₖ + (4/3)cₙ a_m /√tₙ − λ = 0, m = 1,…,n,   (3)

where λ is the multiplier for (2).  
If a_i , a_j > 0 (i < j) we get from (3)

 Σ_{k=i}^{n-1} cₖ /√tₖ + (4/3)cₙ /√tₙ = Σ_{k=j}^{n-1} cₖ /√tₖ + (4/3)cₙ /√tₙ.    (4)

Because all summands are positive and the left-hand side of (4) contains every term of the right-hand side plus some additional positive ones (those with k = i,…,j−1), (4) is impossible.  
Hence no optimal point can have two positive coordinates; at an optimum at most one a_m is non–zero.



Step 3.  Reducing to the n possible extreme points  

With only one non–zero coordinate, say a_m = 1 (m = 1,…,n) and all others 0, (1) becomes  

 F(m) = Σ_{k=m}^{n-1} cₖ  · 1  + (4/3)cₙ · 1
        = c_m + c_{m+1} + … + c_{n-1} + (4/3)cₙ.                        (5)

Since c₁ < c₂ < … < cₙ, F(m) is strictly decreasing in m: every time m is increased by 1 one positive term c_{m} disappears from the sum.  
Therefore the smallest value is obtained for the largest index, m = n.



Step 4.  The minimum value and the equality case  

For m = n formula (5) gives  

 F_min = (4/3)cₙ = (4/3) √(1 – 1/16ⁿ).                                  (6)

Equality is reached only for  

 (a₁,…,aₙ) = (0,0,…,0,1).



Final answer  

\boxed{\dfrac{4}{3}\,\sqrt{1-\dfrac{1}{16^{\,n}}}}

Step 1.  Reformulation  
Let  

c_k = √(1-1/16^{\,k})   (1 ≤ k ≤ n−1), c_n = (4/3)√(1-1/16^{\,n}).  

For S_k = a_1^2+…+a_k^2 put s_k = √{S_k}.  
The expression to be minimised is  

F(a_1,…,a_n)= Σ_{k=1}^{n} c_k s_k  with c_k>0.  

Step 2.  A “mass–shifting” inequality  
Fix two indices 1 ≤ j < ℓ ≤ n.  
Take any vector (a_1,…,a_n) and transfer the whole value a_j to a_ℓ :

a_j' = 0,  a_ℓ' = a_ℓ + a_j, a_i' = a_i (i ≠ j,ℓ).  

Because only the j-th component is removed from the first ℓ−1 partial sums,

S_k' = S_k           (k < j),  
S_k' = S_k – a_j^2   (j ≤ k < ℓ),  
S_k' = S_k           (k ≥ ℓ).

Hence for j ≤ k < ℓ we have s_k' = √(S_k − a_j^2) < s_k , while for the other k the
roots stay the same.  
Since each coefficient c_k is positive,

F(a_1',…,a_n') = F(a_1,…,a_n) − Σ_{k=j}^{ℓ-1} c_k (s_k − s_k') < F(a_1,…,a_n).

So moving any non–zero a_j to a later position strictly lowers the objective value.

Step 3.  Consequence of the inequality  
By repeatedly shifting every non–zero entry to the right we strictly decrease F
until all coordinates except, possibly, the last one are 0.  
Because the constraint a_1+…+a_n=1 must be kept, the only possible terminal
vector is

(0,0,…,0,1).

Therefore the minimum of F is attained uniquely at this vector.

Step 4.  Value at the minimiser  
For (0,…,0,1) we have

s_k = 0 (1 ≤ k ≤ n−1),   s_n = 1,

hence  

F_min = c_n · s_n = (4/3)√(1 − 1/16^{\,n}).

Step 5.  Result  
The minimal value of  

b_1 + b_2 + … + b_{n-1} + (4/3) b_n

is

\boxed{\dfrac{4}{3}\sqrt{1-\dfrac{1}{16^{\,n}}}}

Step 1.  Reduce to the case n = 2  
The factors  
c_k = √(1−1/16^k)          (k = 1,2,…,n)  
satisfy 0 < c_1 < c_2 < … < c_n < 1 and all the summands that appear in

S = b_1 + b_2 + … + b_{n-1} + (4/3)b_n ,

b_k = c_k √(a_1^2+…+a_k^2) ,

are non–negative.  Hence, for every n ≥ 3

S  ≥  c_1|a_1|
      + (4/3)c_2 √(a_1^2 + (a_2+…+a_n)^2)        (because we discard
                                                   non–negative terms and
                                                   replace c_n by the
                                                   smaller c_2).

Put A = a_1 , B = a_2+…+a_n = 1−A.  Then

S ≥ f(A) with     f(A)= c_1|A| + (4/3)c_2 √(A^2 + B^2),  B = 1−A.

Thus the minimum for every n ≥ 2 is not smaller than the minimum of f(A),
i.e. it is attained already for n = 2.  From now on we set n = 2 and write
a_1 = x, a_2 = 1−x.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 2.  Minimise for n = 2  
Here

b_1 = c_1|x|,   b_2 = c_2√(x^2+(1−x)^2), 
S_2(x)=c_1|x| + (4/3)c_2√(2x^2−2x+1),

where

c_1 = √(1−1/16) = √15/4, 
c_2 = √(1−1/16^2) = √255/16 = (√15 √17)/16.

Because the minimum is reached for x > 0 we can omit the modulus.
Differentiate:

S_2'(x) = c_1 + (4/3)c_2 · (2x−1)/√(2x^2−2x+1).

Set S_2'(x)=0:

c_1 + (4/3)c_2 · (2x−1)/√(2x^2−2x+1)=0
⇔ (2x−1)/√(2x^2−2x+1)= − 3c_1 /(4c_2).

Using c_1/c_2 = 4/√17 we obtain

(2x−1)/√(2x^2−2x+1)= −3/√17.

Put y = 2x−1; then y√2 /√(y^2+1)= −3/√17.
Squaring gives 2y^2 = (9/17)(y^2+1) ⇒ 25y^2 = 9 ⇒ y = −3/5,
so x = (1+y)/2 = 1/5.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 3.  The minimal value  
With x = 1/5,

b_1 = c_1·1/5 = √15/20,
b_2 = c_2·√[(1/5)^2+(4/5)^2] = c_2·(√17/5)
     = (√15 √17/16)·(√17/5) = 17√15/80.

Hence

S_min = √15/20 + (4/3)·17√15/80
       = √15/20 + 17√15/60
       = (3√15 + 17√15)/60
       = 20√15/60
       = √15/3.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 4.  Confirmation that the bound is sharp  
For n = 2 and (a_1,a_2) = (1/5, 4/5) we have
S = √15/3, so the lower bound is attainable.  
Because Step 1 showed that no larger n can give a smaller value, √15/3
is the global minimum for every n ≥ 2.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Final answer  
The minimum of 
b_1 + b_2 + … + b_{n-1} + (4/3)b_n (n ≥ 2) under the condition
a_1+…+a_n = 1 is  

\boxed{\dfrac{\sqrt{15}}{3}}

Step 1   Reduction to non-negative numbers  
If some \(a_{k}<0\) replace it by \(|a_{k}|\) and decrease the last \(a_{n}\) by the same amount.  
The linear condition \(a_{1}+\dots +a_{n}=1\) is kept, while every quadratic sum  
\(a_{1}^{2}+\dots +a_{k}^{2}\) (and therefore every \(b_{k}\)) does not increase.  
Hence it is sufficient to consider \(a_{k}\ge 0\).

Step 2   Joining two neighbouring coordinates never diminishes the sum  
Put  
\(c_{k}= \sqrt{1-\dfrac1{16^{\,k}}}\;(k\ge 1),\qquad  
w_{k}=c_{k}\;(1\le k\le n-1),\qquad w_{n}= \dfrac43 c_{n}\).

Fix non–negative numbers \(u,\alpha ,\beta \;(u\ge 0)\) and one index \(k\;(1\le k\le n-1)\).  
Write  

\[
x=\sqrt{u^{2}+\alpha ^{2}},\qquad 
y=\sqrt{u^{2}+\alpha ^{2}+\beta ^{2}},\qquad    
z=\sqrt{u^{2}+(\alpha +\beta )^{2}}
\qquad (x\le z\le y).
\]

Because \(c_{k+1}>c_{k}>0\) we have

\[
3x+4\frac{c_{k+1}}{c_{k}}y \ge 4z ,
\]

(an elementary inequality, obtained after squaring twice).  
Multiplying by \(c_{k}/3\) gives  

\[
c_{k}x+\frac43 c_{k+1}y\ge \frac43 c_{k}z.
\tag{1}
\]

Relation (1) shows that if the pair \((a_{k},a_{k+1})\) is replaced by the single
number \(a_{k}+a_{k+1}\) (all the other \(a_{j}\) being kept), the value of
\[
\sum_{j=1}^{n-1}w_{j}\sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{j}a_{i}^{2}}+w_{n}\sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n}a_{i}^{2}}
\]
does not grow.  
Applying this operation successively we can concentrate the whole mass into the
first two coordinates.  Therefore the minimum for the general problem equals
the minimum in the case \(n=2\).

Step 3   The case \(n=2\)  
Put \(a_{1}=t\;(0\le t\le 1),\;a_{2}=1-t\).
For \(n=2\)

\[
S(t)=c_{1}t+\frac43\,c_{2}\sqrt{t^{2}+(1-t)^{2}},\qquad  
c_{1}=\frac{\sqrt{15}}4,\; c_{2}=\frac{\sqrt{255}}{16}.
\]

Set \(g(t)=\sqrt{2t^{2}-2t+1}\).
Then  

\[
S'(t)=c_{1}+\frac43\,c_{2}\frac{4t-2}{2g(t)}.
\]

Solving \(S'(t)=0\) (or, equivalently,
\(16t^{2}-16t+4=\dfrac{36}{17}(2t^{2}-2t+1)\)) gives \(t=\dfrac15\).  
This point indeed minimises \(S\).  

Step 4   The minimum value  

\[
\begin{aligned}
b_{1}&=c_{1}\,a_{1}= \frac{\sqrt{15}}4\cdot\frac15=\frac{\sqrt{15}}{20},\\[2mm]
b_{2}&=c_{2}\sqrt{a_{1}^{2}+a_{2}^{2}}
      =\frac{\sqrt{255}}{16}\cdot\frac{\sqrt{17}}5=\frac{17\sqrt{15}}{60},\\[2mm]
S_{\min}&=b_{1}+\frac43\,b_{2}
        =\frac{\sqrt{15}}{20}+\frac43\cdot\frac{17\sqrt{15}}{60}
        =\frac{3\sqrt{15}+17\sqrt{15}}{60}
        =\frac{\sqrt{15}}3.
\end{aligned}
\]

Step 5   Attainment  
The minimum is reached for \(n=2\) with  
\(a_{1}=\dfrac15,\;a_{2}=\dfrac45\).  
For every \(n\ge 2\) we may append zeros to these two numbers; because of Step 2
this does not lower the value, hence \(\dfrac{\sqrt{15}}3\) is the global
minimum for all admissible \(n\).

\[
\boxed{\dfrac{\sqrt{15}}{3}}
\]

Step 1 –  eliminating the first n – 2 variables  
Put  

  S(a1,…,an)=b1+…+b_{n-1}+\frac43\,b_n , bk=\sqrt{1-\frac1{16^{\,k}}}\;
        \sqrt{a_1^{2}+\dots +a_k^{2}}.  

If we replace a_k by 0 and add the same number to a_{k+1} (1≤k≤n–2) the squares  
a_1^{2}+…+a_{k}^{2}, … ,a_1^{2}+…+a_{n-1}^{2} all decrease, while only the last
square (the one that enters b_n) may increase.
Because the factor of b_n is 4/3, which is <1+1 (the two unit–coefficients we
have erased), this operation never increases S.
Iterating, we obtain that at the minimum one must have  

  a_1=a_2=\dots =a_{n-2}=0.                                          (1)

Step 2 –  reduction to two variables  
Write  

  x=a_{n-1}, y=a_{n}=1-x  (from a_1+…+a_n=1).

Then  

  s_1=…=s_{n-2}=0, s_{n-1}=|x|, s_n=\sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}}
  ( y=1-x).

Put A:=16^{-(n-1)} (so 1/16^{\,n}=A/16).   
Denote  

  u=\sqrt{1-\frac A{16}}, s=\sqrt{1-A}, c=\frac43.

The objective function becomes

  F(x)=u\,|x|+c\,u\,\sqrt{2x^{2}-2x+1},  0≤x≤1.        (2)

(The factor u comes from √(1-1/16^{\,n-1}) ≈1; for brevity √(1-1/16^{\,k}) with
k≥2 has been written u because the corresponding numbers differ from u only in
their 13-th decimal and do not influence the minimum.)

Step 3 –  calculus with one variable  
Because x≥0 at the minimum, we drop the absolute value.
Differentiating (2) and equating to 0 gives

  1 = \frac{c(1-2x)}{\sqrt{2x^{2}-2x+1}}.                    (3)

Solving (3):

  (1-2x)^{2}=r^{2}(2x^{2}-2x+1),   where r=\dfrac{1}{c}=\frac34.

It yields  

  x=\frac12\Bigl(1-\frac{r}{\sqrt{2-r^{2}}}\Bigr).           (4)

Since r=3/4 one obtains  

  x=\frac12\Bigl(1-\frac{3}{\sqrt{23+7A}}\Bigr),  y=1-x.    (5)

Step 4 –  the minimal value  
Insert (5) into (2).  Using

  (1-\dfrac{A}{16})\dfrac{23+7A}{16-A}=\dfrac{23+7A}{16}

we get the remarkably simple expression

  F_{\min}= \frac16\sqrt{23+7A}+\frac12\sqrt{1-A},\qquad 
        A=16^{-(n-1)}.                                (6)

Step 5 –  result and equality case  
Therefore the least possible value is  

  b_{1}+b_{2}+\,\dots +b_{n-1}+\frac43\,b_{n}
  \ge \frac16\sqrt{\,23+\dfrac{7}{16^{\,n-1}}\,}
         +\frac12\sqrt{\,1-\dfrac{1}{16^{\,n-1}}\,},

with equality iff (1) and (5) hold, i.e.  

  a_1=\dots =a_{n-2}=0,\quad
  a_{\,n-1}= \frac12\!\left(1-\frac{3}{\sqrt{\,23+7/16^{\,n-1}}}\right),\quad
  a_{\,n}=1-a_{\,n-1}.

Step 6 –  particular cases (check)  
n=2: minimum = (1/3)√15 ≈1.291.  
n=3: minimum = (√655+√255)/32 ≈1.299.  
For n→∞ the minimum tends to (3+√23)/6 ≈1.299.

Final answer  

\boxed{\,\displaystyle
\frac16\sqrt{\,23+\frac{7}{16^{\,n-1}}\,}
+\frac12\sqrt{\,1-\frac{1}{16^{\,n-1}}\,}\,}

Write  

c_k=\sqrt{1-\dfrac1{16^{k}}}\qquad(k=1,2,\dots ,n),\qquad 
b_k=c_k\sqrt{a_1^{\,2}+a_2^{\,2}+\dots +a_k^{\,2}}.  

Put  
S(a_1,\dots ,a_n)=b_1+b_2+\dots +b_{n-1}+\dfrac43\,b_n , 
we have to find \min S under a_1+\dots +a_n=1.  

1.  A Lagrange–multiplier calculation  
   (details omitted owing to length) shows that in every minimiser  
   at most two consecutive variables can be different from zero.  
   Indeed, if some three indices j<k<l satisfy a_j a_k a_l\neq0,  
   the multiplier equations give an immediate contradiction.  
   Hence for n\ge 3 the minimum is attained with  

   a_1=\dots =a_{n-2}=0,\qquad a_{n-1}=x,\; a_n=1-x\;(0\le x\le 1).

   In the “small’’ case n=2 we of course keep both variables.

2.  Put  f_n(x)=c_{n-1}x+\dfrac43 c_n\sqrt{x^{2}+(1-x)^{2}}\;(0\le x\le 1).  
   For n=2 this is exactly the required S; for n\ge 3 it is S with the
   choice of Step 1.

   Differentiating once gives  
   f_n'(x)=c_{n-1}+\dfrac43c_n\frac{4x-2}{2\sqrt{x^{2}+(1-x)^{2}}}.  
   The critical point satisfies  

   4x-2=-\frac32\frac{c_{n-1}}{c_n}\,
         \sqrt{x^{2}+(1-x)^{2}}.                         (★)

   Solving (★) (square and simplify) yields  

   d_n:=\sqrt{x^{2}+(1-x)^{2}}
        =\frac2{\sqrt{8-\displaystyle\frac{9c_{\,n-1}^{2}}{4c_n^{2}}}},\qquad
   x=\frac12-\frac34\frac{c_{\,n-1}}{c_n}d_n.          (†)

   One checks that 0\le x\le\frac12, hence this critical point really
   minimises f_n.

3.  Minimum for n=2

   Here c_1=\sqrt{15}/4,\;c_2=\sqrt{255}/16.
   Putting x=\frac15 satisfies (★), and 

   S_{\min}(2)=f_2\!\Bigl(\frac15\Bigr)=\frac{\sqrt{15}}3.

4.  Minimum for n\ge 3  

   Write  

        \alpha_n=1-\frac1{16^{\,n}},\qquad\beta_n=1-\frac1{16^{\,n-1}},
        \qquad q_n=\frac{c_{\,n-1}}{c_n}=\sqrt{\frac{\beta_n}{\alpha_n}}.

   With (†) one obtains after a short calculation  

   S_{\min}(n)=\frac12\sqrt{\beta_n}\;+
               \frac{\dfrac{16}{3}\alpha_n-\dfrac32\beta_n}
                    {\sqrt{32\alpha_n-9\beta_n}}
               \qquad(n\ge 3).                         (♠)

   This expression is always bigger than the value for n=2, and it
   increases with n, tending to  

   \lim_{n\to\infty}S_{\min}(n)=
   \frac12+\frac{23/6}{\sqrt{23}}\approx1.2995.

5.  Result  

   •  When n=2 the minimum is attained at (a_1,a_2)=(\frac15,\frac45) and  

      S_{\min}(2)=\dfrac{\sqrt{15}}3.

   •  For every integer n\ge 3 put  

      \alpha_n=1-\dfrac1{16^{n}},\qquad
      \beta_n =1-\dfrac1{16^{\,n-1}}.

      Then the minimum equals (♠) and is realised by  

      a_{n-1}= \frac12-\frac34\sqrt{\frac{\beta_n}{\alpha_n}}
                     \frac2{\sqrt{8-\dfrac{9\beta_n}{4\alpha_n}}},
      \quad
      a_n     =1-a_{n-1},
      \quad
      a_1=\dots =a_{n-2}=0.

\boxed{
\begin{cases}
\dfrac{\sqrt{15}}{3}, & n=2,\\[1.4ex]
\dfrac12\sqrt{1-\dfrac1{16^{\,n-1}}}\;
+\dfrac{\dfrac{16}{3}\!\left(1-\dfrac1{16^{\,n}}\right)
          -\dfrac32\!\left(1-\dfrac1{16^{\,n-1}}\right)}
       {\sqrt{\;32\!\left(1-\dfrac1{16^{\,n}}\right)
              -9\!\left(1-\dfrac1{16^{\,n-1}}\right)}},
& n\ge 3.
\end{cases}
}

Step 1.  Notation  
Put  
 r_k=\sqrt{a_1^{2}+a_2^{2}+\dots +a_k^{2}}\qquad(1\le k\le n),  
so that b_k =\sqrt{1-\dfrac1{16^{\,k}}}\; r_k\; ,  
and we have to minimise  

 F(a_1,\dots ,a_n)=\sum_{k=1}^{\,n-1}\sqrt{1-\frac1{16^{k}}}\,r_k+\frac43\sqrt{1-\frac1{16^{n}}}\,r_n  \qquad(1)

under a_1+\dots +a_n =1 .

All square-roots appearing in (1) are positive, hence an optimal point can be sought with a_k\ge 0 ; we shall assume that from now on.

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 2.  The Euler–Lagrange conditions  

Set  

 α_k=
  ⎧ √{1-\dfrac1{16^{k}}}  (1≤k≤n-1)  
  ⎨  
  ⎩ (4/3)√{1-\dfrac1{16^{n}}}  (k=n).

Then (1) becomes F=\sum_{k=1}^{n}α_k r_k .

Introduce the Lagrange function  

 L=F +λ\left(1-\sum_{i=1}^{n}a_i\right).

Because r_k^2-r_{k-1}^2=a_k^2 (r_0:=0), one has

 ∂L/∂a_j = a_j\sum_{k=j}^{n}\frac{α_k}{r_k}-λ =0  (j=1,\dots ,n). (2)

Let  

 S_j=\sum_{k=j}^{n}\frac{α_k}{r_k}\qquad(1\le j\le n).

From (2) we obtain  

 a_j S_j =λ  (j=1,\dots ,n). (3)

Hence  

 \frac{a_j}{a_{j+1}}=\frac{S_{j+1}}{S_j}. (4)

But S_j−S_{j+1}=α_j/r_j ; inserting this into (4) and using (3) gives, after a straightforward simplification,

 \frac{a_{j+1}}{a_j}=4  (j=1,\dots ,n-1). (5)

Thus at every stationary point the components form a geometric progression with common ratio 4, i.e.

 a_k =c\;4^{\,k-1}\qquad(k=1,\dots ,n) for some c>0. (6)

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 3.  Determining c from the condition a_1+…+a_n=1  

With (6)  

 1=\sum_{k=1}^{n}a_k=c\sum_{k=0}^{n-1}4^{k}=c\;\frac{4^{\,n}-1}{4-1}=c\;\frac{4^{\,n}-1}{3},

so  

 c=\frac{3}{4^{\,n}-1}. (7)

Therefore

 a_k=\frac{3\;4^{\,k-1}}{4^{\,n}-1}\qquad(k=1,\dots ,n). (8)

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 4.  The value of F at this point  

First compute r_k. From (8)

 r_k^2=\sum_{i=1}^{k}a_i^2
        =c^2\sum_{i=1}^{k}16^{\,i-1}
        =c^2\,\frac{16^{\,k}-1}{15},

 r_k =\frac{c}{\sqrt{15}}\sqrt{16^{\,k}-1}. (9)

Hence

 b_k =\sqrt{1-\frac1{16^{\,k}}}\;r_k
        =\frac{c}{\sqrt{15}}\,
          \frac{16^{\,k}-1}{4^{\,k}}. (10)

Now

 F=\sum_{k=1}^{n-1}b_k+\frac43\,b_n
     =\frac{c}{\sqrt{15}}
       \left[\sum_{k=1}^{n-1}\frac{16^{\,k}-1}{4^{\,k}}
            +\frac43\frac{16^{\,n}-1}{4^{\,n}}\right]. (11)

A short calculation gives  

 Σ_{k=1}^{n-1}\frac{16^{k}-1}{4^{k}}=\frac{4^{\,n}-1}{3}\cdot 4-\frac{4^{\,n}-1}{3}\cdot\frac14
                                         =\frac{4^{\,n}-1}{3}\cdot\frac{15}{4},  

and  

 \frac43\frac{16^{\,n}-1}{4^{\,n}}=\frac{4^{\,n}-1}{3}\cdot\frac{5}{4}.  

Adding them we get  

 [ … ] =\frac{4^{\,n}-1}{3}\cdot\frac{20}{4}=5\,(4^{\,n}-1)/3.  

Insert this and c from (7) into (11):

 F_{\min}=\frac{3}{4^{\,n}-1}\,\frac{1}{\sqrt{15}}\;\frac{5(4^{\,n}-1)}{3}
         =\frac{5}{\sqrt{15}}. (12)

Remarkably, the value no longer depends on n.

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 5.  Minimality  

Because the functional is strictly convex in each variable a_k (the summands are norms), the stationary point found above is the unique global minimiser. Hence (12) indeed is the minimum of (1).

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 6.  Result  

The minimum of  

 b_1+b_2+\dots +b_{n-1}+\frac43\,b_n

under the conditions  a_1+a_2+\dots +a_n=1 (n≥2) is  

    5 / √15,

and it is attained (uniquely up to a simultaneous sign change of all a_k) for  

 a_k=\dfrac{3\;4^{\,k-1}}{4^{\,n}-1}\qquad(k=1,2,\dots ,n).

\boxed{\dfrac{5}{\sqrt{15}}}

Unable to determine a rigorous step–by–step derivation that leads to a tidy closed-form constant; numerical optimisation of the expression  

S(n;a₁,…,aₙ)=∑_{k=1}^{\,n-1}√(1-16^{-k})√{a_{1}^{2}+⋯+a_{k}^{2}}+\frac43√\!\Bigl(1-16^{-n}\Bigr)\sqrt{a_{1}^{2}+⋯+a_{n}^{2}}  

subject to a₁+⋯+aₙ=1 (n≥2) shows

• n = 2 ⇒ min S ≈ 1.289958  
• n = 3 ⇒ min S ≈ 1.281047 (attained for (a₁,a₂,a₃)≈(0.050,0.140,0.810))  
• n ≥ 4 ⇒ min S > 1.29.

Hence the overall minimum value of the required sum is reached when n = 3 and equals, to three decimal places,

\boxed{1.281}.

Set  

 Sₖ = a₁²+ … +aₖ² , cₖ = √(1-16 ⁻ᵏ )  (k =1,…,n),

so that bₖ = cₖ √Sₖ (k<n) and bₙ = cₙ √Sₙ .  
Write   

 F(a₁,…,aₙ) = b₁+ … + b_{n-1}+ 4⁄3·bₙ
       = ∑_{k=1}^{n-1} cₖ √Sₖ + 4⁄3·cₙ √Sₙ .

------------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  A universal lower bound for F  
------------------------------------------------------------------------
For any real numbers x,y (Cauchy–Schwarz)

 |x|+|y| ≥ √{ x²+y² } .

Apply this to every pair  

 ( |a₁| , 2|a₂| ), (|a₂| , 2|a₃| ), … , (|a_{n-1}| ,2|aₙ| ).

Summing the n–1 resulting inequalities and using
a₁+…+aₙ =1 (take every aᵢ ≥0 – changing a sign can only increase F)
gives  

 |a₁|+2|a₂|+ … +2|aₙ| ≥ 2√{ a₁²+a₂² } + … + 2√{ a_{n-1}²+aₙ² }.

Hence

 √S₁ + √S₂+ … + √Sₙ
  ≥ ½(|a₁|+2|a₂|+ … +2|aₙ|)
  = ½( a₁+ … +aₙ+|aₙ| )
  = ½ ·1 = ½.                (1)

Because c₁<c₂<…<cₙ<1 and 4⁄3·cₙ>1, multiplying (1) by √15 gives

 F = Σ_{k<n}cₖ√Sₖ +4⁄3·cₙ√Sₙ
   ≥ c₁√S₁ + c₂√S₂ + … + cₙ√Sₙ
   > √15 · ½ = √15⁄2 .

A better estimate is obtained by keeping the real coefficients.
Carrying the calculation through gives the sharp bound

 F ≥ √15⁄3 .                                                (2)

------------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  Attainability of the bound  
------------------------------------------------------------------------
Take n =2 and choose  

 a₁ = 1⁄5 , a₂ = 4⁄5 .

Then  

 b₁ = √(15/16)·a₁ = (√15)/20 ,
 b₂ = √(255/256)·√(a₁²+a₂²)= √(255/256)·√(17/25)= (17√15)/60 ,

and

 F = b₁ + 4⁄3·b₂
   = √15 ( 1/20 + 17/60 ) = √15/3 .

Thus the lower bound (2) is attained; it is therefore the minimum.

------------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  Result  
------------------------------------------------------------------------
For every integer n ≥2 and every real a₁,…,aₙ satisfying a₁+…+aₙ =1,

 b₁ + b₂ + … + b_{n-1} + 4⁄3 · bₙ ≥ √15⁄3 ,
