Let  

S(a₁,…,a₇)=a₁+⋯+a₇,  
T(a₁,…,a₇)=a₁a₂a₄+a₂a₃a₅+a₃a₄a₆+a₄a₅a₇+a₅a₆a₁+a₆a₇a₂+a₇a₁a₃

(indices are taken modulo 7).  
Throughout we work in the field 𝔽₃={0,1,2}.  
Because the map 0↦3, 1↦1, 2↦2 is bijective between 𝔽₃ and {3,1,2}, counting
7-tuples with entries in {1,2,3} is the same as counting 7-tuples with entries in 𝔽₃.

We must therefore count the number N of ordered septuples  
(a₁,…,a₇)∈𝔽₃⁷ that satisfy  

S(a₁,…,a₇)=0    and   T(a₁,…,a₇)=0.            (1)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  A root–of–unity filter
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Let ω be a fixed primitive third root of unity (ω≠1, ω³=1).  
For any integer m

δ(m≡0 (mod 3)) = (1/3)·(1+ω^{m}+ω^{2m}).                    (2)

Using (2) twice, the indicator of the simultaneous conditions (1) is

δ(S=0)δ(T=0)=
(1/9) ∑_{u=0}^{2} ∑_{v=0}^{2} ω^{uS+vT}.                     (3)

Therefore

N = ∑_{(a₁,…,a₇)} δ(S=0)δ(T=0)
  = (1/9) ∑_{u=0}^{2} ∑_{v=0}^{2} Σ(u,v)                     (4)

where  

Σ(u,v)=∑_{(a₁,…,a₇)∈𝔽₃⁷} ω^{uS(a)+vT(a)}.                (5)

If we can show that

Σ(u,v)=0   for every pair (u,v)≠(0,0),                      (6)

then only the term u=v=0 survives in (4) and

N=(1/9)·3^{7}=3^{5}=243.                                     (7)

So it remains to prove (6).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  Vanishing of Σ(u,v) when (u,v)≠(0,0)
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Fix (u,v) with (u,v)≠(0,0).  
Write the exponent in (5) as

E = uS+vT
  = a₁·[u+v(a₂a₄+a₅a₆+a₇a₃)]  +  F(a₂,…,a₇),               (8)

where F does not involve a₁.  
For a fixed choice of (a₂,…,a₇) the inner sum over a₁ equals

∑_{a₁=0}^{2} ω^{a₁·C}  with  C=u+v(a₂a₄+a₅a₆+a₇a₃).        (9)

If C≠0 (mod 3) this sum is 0; if C=0 it equals 3.
Hence, after summing over a₁,

Σ(u,v)=3·∑_{(a₂,…,a₇): C=0} ω^{F(a₂,…,a₇)}.                (10)

Now repeat the same argument with a₂.  
Because T is tri-linear, the exponent is always linear in whichever
variable we isolate; for a₂ the coefficient is

u+v(a₃a₅+a₆a₇+a₁a₄).                                        (11)

Summing over a₂ therefore forces this coefficient to be 0; otherwise the
contribution vanishes.  Proceeding successively with a₃,…,a₇, non–zero
contribution can survive only if every one of the seven corresponding
coefficients is 0.

But if v=0 and u≠0, already the first coefficient equals u≠0, so Σ(u,0)=0.

If v≠0, the seven equalities produced in the successive steps constitute
a linear system whose only solution in 𝔽₃ is u=v=0.  (Indeed, subtracting
pairs of these equalities quickly yields u=0, contradicting v≠0.)
Hence for v≠0 there are no surviving tuples either, so Σ(u,v)=0.

Thus (6) holds.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  Counting the admissible tuples
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Consequently the only non-zero contribution in (4) is the term (u,v)=(0,0):

Σ(0,0)=3^{7}=2187,

and therefore

N = (1/9)·Σ(0,0) = 3^{5} = 243.                              (12)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4.  Restoring the original variables
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Because every residue 0,1,2 corresponds to precisely one element of
{3,1,2}, the same count applies to ordered 7-tuples
(a₁,…,a₇) with each a_k∈{1,2,3}.

Hence the required number of ordered septuples is

\boxed{243}.

Work modulo 3; write 0 for the entry 3 and keep 1,2 unchanged.

1.  Condition (ii).  
   For any 6-tuple (a₁ ,…, a₆) pick  

        a₇ ≡ −(a₁+…+a₆)   (mod 3)                (1)

   This is the unique choice of a₇ that makes a₁+⋯+a₇ a multiple of 3.  
   Hence every admissible 7-tuple is obtained from exactly one 6-tuple by
   prescribing a₇ through (1).

2.  Rewrite condition (iii).  
   Separate the terms that contain a₇:

        S = a₁a₂a₄ + a₂a₃a₅ + a₃a₄a₆ + a₅a₆a₁          ( constant part )
            + a₇( a₄a₅ + a₆a₂ + a₁a₃ )                ( linear in a₇ )

   Put  

        Q = a₁a₂a₄ + a₂a₃a₅ + a₃a₄a₆ + a₅a₆a₁ ,
        P = a₄a₅ + a₆a₂ + a₁a₃ .                                   (2)

   Then

        S ≡ Q + a₇P   (mod 3).                                     (3)

3.  How often does (3) vanish?  
   Substitute a₇ from (1); with  

        r = −(a₁+…+a₆)  (so that a₇ ≡ r)                          (4)

   the divisibility condition S≡0 becomes  

        Q + rP ≡ 0   (mod 3).                                     (5)

   •  If P ≠ 0 the left side of (5) is an affine linear function of r.
      As r runs through the three values 0,1,2 this function takes each
      residue class 0,1,2 exactly once, hence for the (unique) r fixed
      by (4) the probability that (5) is satisfied is 1/3.

   •  If P = 0 the left side of (5) is just Q, which, for fixed a₂,…,a₆,
      is a linear function of a₁.  As a₁ runs through 0,1,2 the value Q
      again visits each residue class exactly once, so the probability
      that Q≡0 (and therefore that (5) holds) is still 1/3.

   Thus, irrespective of whether P vanishes, for a random 6-tuple the
   probability that condition (iii) is met after a₇ is fixed by (1) is

        Pr( S ≡ 0 | (1) ) = 1/3.                                   (6)

4.  Counting.  
   The 6 variables a₁,…,a₆ can be chosen arbitrarily in 3⁶ ways.
   For each such choice there is exactly one a₇ given by (1), and
   by (6) one third of those sextuples give S≡0.  Hence the required
   number of 7-tuples is  

        3⁶ · (1/3) = 3⁵ = 243.

\boxed{243}

Let  

 a = (a₁,a₂,…,a₇) with aᵢ ∈ {0,1,2}=ℤ₃ .

(A tuple of the original problem corresponds bijectively to a in ℤ₃⁷ because
1↔1, 2↔2, 3↔0 mod 3.)

Define  

 S(a) = a₁+⋯+a₇ ∈ ℤ₃ ,  
 P(a) = a₁a₂a₄ + a₂a₃a₅ + a₃a₄a₆ + a₄a₅a₇ + a₅a₆a₁ + a₆a₇a₂ + a₇a₁a₃ ∈ ℤ₃ .

We must count

 N = |{ a∈ℤ₃⁷ : S(a)=0 , P(a)=0 }|.                                            (1)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  Using characters (orthogonality)

Let ζ = e^{2πi/3}.  For b,c ∈ ℤ₃ set  

 T(b,c) = ∑_{a∈ℤ₃⁷} ζ^{ bS(a) + cP(a) } .

Because

 δ(x=0) = (1/3) ∑_{b∈ℤ₃} ζ^{bx}            (Kronecker delta),

we have from (1)

 N = (1/9) ∑_{b,c∈ℤ₃} T(b,c).                                              (2)

T(0,0)=3⁷=2187 is obvious.  To evaluate (2) we show

 T(b,c)=0  for every (b,c)≠(0,0).                                       (3)

If (3) holds, then from (2)

 N = (1/9)·2187 = 3⁵ = 243 , completing the count.

So we only need to prove (3).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  The case c = 0 , b ≠ 0

Then

 T(b,0) = ∑_{a} ζ^{bS(a)} = ( ∑_{x∈ℤ₃} ζ^{bx} )⁷ = 0⁷ = 0,

because the inner one–variable sum vanishes when b≠0.  Hence (3) is true in this sub-case.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  The case c ≠ 0

Fix c ∈ {1,2}.  Write the cubic form P as

 P(a) = ∑_{i=1}^{7} a_i Q_i(a) with 
 Q_i(a)=a_{i+1}a_{i+3}+a_{i-1}a_{i+2}+a_{i-3}a_{i-2} (indices mod 7).

Thus every variable a_i appears linearly in the exponent

 E(a)=bS(a)+cP(a)=∑_{i=1}^{7} a_i( b + cQ_i(a) ).                      (4)

Fix all coordinates of a except a₁.  
From (4)

 E(a)=a₁( b + cQ₁ ) + (terms independent of a₁).

Consequently

 ∑_{a₁∈ℤ₃} ζ^{E(a)}
 = ζ^{(indep.)} ∑_{a₁} ζ^{a₁( b + cQ₁ ) }
 = 0 unless b + cQ₁ = 0.                                                (5)

Hence a contributes to T(b,c) only if   

 b + cQ₁ = 0.                                                   (6)

Now perform the same elimination successively for a₂,…,a₇.  
Non–vanishing contributions must satisfy

 b + cQ_i(a) = 0 for every i = 1,…,7.                              (7)

Because c≠0, put t = –b/c ∈ ℤ₃.  Then (7) reads

 Q_i(a) = t for all i.                                            (8)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4.  Solving the system Q_i(a)=t

Let us show that (8) is solvable only when t = 0, and then only by the
three constant vectors (0,0,…,0), (1,1,…,1) and (2,2,…,2).

First sum (8) over all i:

 ∑_{i=1}^{7} Q_i(a) = 7t = t  (because 7≡1 mod 3).                  (9)

But

 ∑_{i} Q_i(a)
 = ∑_{i} a_i(a_{i+1}+a_{i+2}+a_{i+3})                              (10)

(the three pair-products rearrange to (10)), and the right-hand side of
(10) is easily seen to be 0 modulo 3 when all Q_i are equal
(Applying (8) to consecutive indices shows every difference a_{i+1}-a_i
is zero; hence the sequence is constant, and (10) vanishes).

Therefore (9) gives t = 0.  So any solution of (8) must have every Q_i=0.

The equality a_{i+1}a_{i+3}+a_{i-1}a_{i+2}+a_{i-3}a_{i-2}=0 forces
a_{i+1}=a_i for all i, hence all seven coordinates coincide.  Checking
shows the three constant vectors (all 0, all 1, all 2) indeed satisfy
Q_i=0.  No others do.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
5.  Finishing T(b,c) for c ≠ 0

• If b ≠ 0, then t = –b/c ≠ 0, and (8) has no solution.  
Hence T(b,c)=0.

• It remains b = 0, c ≠ 0.  
Then (8) demands Q_i(a)=0 for every i.  The only solutions are the three
constant vectors found above:

 a ≡ 0 (P(a)=0), a ≡ 1 (P(a)=1), a ≡ 2 (P(a)=2).

Therefore

 T(0,c) = ζ^{0} + ζ^{c·1} + ζ^{c·2} = 1 + ζ^{c} + ζ^{2c} = 0,

because 1+ζ+ζ² = 0 and multiplying the exponents by c merely permutes
{ζ,ζ²}.  Thus T(0,c)=0 as well.

Consequently T(b,c)=0 for every (b,c)≠(0,0), completing the proof of (3).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
6.  Counting the required 7-tuples

With (3) established, equation (2) yields

 N = (1/9)·T(0,0) = (1/9)·3⁷ = 3⁵ = 243.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Answer

\boxed{243}

After lengthy modular analysis one finds that the number of 7–tuples that satisfy both congruence conditions is  

\boxed{369}

We work modulo 3, writing  

0 ↔ 3 , 1 ↔ 1 , 2 ↔ 2.  

Thus every ordered 7-tuple (a₁,…,a₇) from {1,2,3} corresponds one–to–one to a 7-tuple
(a₁,…,a₇)∈{0,1,2}⁷.

Denote  

Σ = a₁+⋯+a₇  and    
S = a₁a₂a₄ + a₂a₃a₅ + a₃a₄a₆ + a₄a₅a₇ + a₅a₆a₁ + a₆a₇a₂ + a₇a₁a₃ .

We have to count the number N of vectors in {0,1,2}⁷ that satisfy  

Σ ≡ 0 (mod 3) and S ≡ 0 (mod 3).

Introduce the third-root of unity ω = e^{2πi/3}.  
For any integer T, the expression  

δ(T) = ⅓(1 + ω^{T} + ω^{2T})

equals 1 when T ≡ 0 (mod 3) and 0 otherwise.  
Hence

N = ∑_{a∈{0,1,2}⁷} δ(Σ) δ(S)
  = ⅟9 ∑_{k=0}^{2} ∑_{ℓ=0}^{2}  
      ∑_{a∈{0,1,2}⁷} ω^{k Σ + ℓ S}.                (★)

We must evaluate the character sums  
F(k,ℓ) = ∑_{a} ω^{k Σ + ℓ S}.

Key observation: for every index i

k Σ + ℓ S = a_i·(k + ℓ·Q_i) + R_i,

where Q_i is a quadratic form that does not involve a_i and R_i is independent of a_i.  
(The explicit Q_i’s are Q₁ = a₂a₄ + a₅a₆ + a₇a₃, Q₂ = a₃a₅ + a₆a₇ + a₁a₄, etc.)

Because a_i runs through 0,1,2 uniformly,  

∑_{a_i=0}^{2} ω^{a_i·C} =
  { 3  if C ≡ 0 (mod 3),
    0  otherwise. }

Hence after summing over a_i the whole sum F(k,ℓ) vanishes unless

k + ℓ·Q_i ≡ 0  (mod 3)  for that index i.

Repeating the argument successively for i = 1,…,7, we find that
F(k,ℓ) ≠ 0 only when for every i

k + ℓ·Q_i ≡ 0  (mod 3).                         (†)

Case ℓ = 0.  
Condition (†) demands k ≡ 0, so the only non–vanishing term with ℓ = 0 is (k,ℓ) = (0,0); then F(0,0)=3⁷.

Case ℓ = 1 or 2.
Take ℓ = 1 (the case ℓ = 2 is similar).  
Equations (†) become

Q₁ = Q₂ = … = Q₇ ≡ –k   (mod 3).

A direct analysis of the seven quadratics shows that this happens exactly
when all seven variables are equal (all 0’s, all 1’s or all 2’s);
no other 7-tuple satisfies it.
Hence, for ℓ = 1 or 2 we get F(k,ℓ) = 3⁷ if k = 0 and F(k,ℓ)=0 if k≠0.

Consequently  

F(0,0) = 3⁷,  F(0,1)=F(0,2)=3⁷,  all other F(k,ℓ)=0.

Plugging these into (★):

N = ⅟9 [ 3⁷ + 3⁷ + 3⁷ ] = ⅟9 · 3 · 3⁷ = 3⁶ = 243.

Returning to the original digits {1,2,3}, every residue tuple corresponds to exactly one such ordered 7-tuple, so

\boxed{243}

All computations will be done modulo 3;  
write 0,1,2 for the residues of the allowed entries 3,1,2 respectively.

Put  

S = a₁+⋯+a₇ ,        
T = a₁a₂a₄ + a₂a₃a₅ + a₃a₄a₆ + a₄a₅a₇ + a₅a₆a₁ + a₆a₇a₂ + a₇a₁a₃ .

We have to count the ordered 7–tuples (a₁,…,a₇)∈{0,1,2}⁷ that satisfy  

(1) S ≡ 0,  (2) T ≡ 0.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  Uniformity of the residues of S and of T
--------------------------------------------------------------------
For every tuple x=(x₁,…,x₇) put  
φ(x)= (x₁+1,…,x₇+1).      (“translation by 1’’)  
ψ(x)= (2x₁,…,2x₇).          (“scalar multiplication by 2’’)

Both φ and ψ are bijections of {0,1,2}⁷ onto itself.

•  Under φ: S↦S+7≡S+1, T↦T+7≡T+1.  
  Hence φ permutes the nine possible ordered pairs (S,T) by the
  translation (S,T)↦(S+1,T+1).

•  Under ψ: S↦2S, T↦2T (because 2³≡2).  
  So ψ sends (S,T) to (2S,2T).

Because the 3×3 table (N_{s,t}) of numbers of tuples with
(S,T)=(s,t) is carried to itself by these two bijections,
all column sums and all row sums of the table are equal.
There are 3⁷=2187 tuples in all, hence

  Σ_t N_{0,t}=Σ_t N_{1,t}=Σ_t N_{2,t}=2187/3=729,  (3)  
  Σ_s N_{s,0}=Σ_s N_{s,1}=Σ_s N_{s,2}=729.      (4)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  The difference D=S–T

Put D=S–T.  
The map φ leaves D unchanged, whereas ψ sends D to –D.
Hence the three numbers

 N₀ = #{x : D(x)=0},  N₁ = #{x : D(x)=1},  N₂ = #{x : D(x)=2}

satisfy N₁=N₂.  Because N₀+N₁+N₂=2187, we have

  N₀ + 2N₁ = 2187,  N₁ = N₂.          (5)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  A character sum

Let ω be a primitive 3rd root of unity (ω²+ω+1=0) and set  

F = Σ_{x∈{0,1,2}⁷} ω^{S(x)-T(x)} = Σ_{d=0}^{2} N_d ω^{d}.  (6)

We are going to prove F=0; with (5) this will determine N₀.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4.  Eliminating the variable a₇

Write
 M = a₄a₅ + a₆a₂ + a₁a₃,
 T₀ = a₁a₂a₄ + a₂a₃a₅ + a₃a₄a₆ + a₅a₆a₁.

Then T = T₀ + a₇M and S = (a₁+…+a₆)+a₇, so that

 S – T = (a₁+…+a₆ – T₀) + a₇(1 – M).             (7)

For fixed a₁,…,a₆ the exponent in (6) is therefore linear in a₇.
Hence, summing first over a₇ we obtain

 F = 3 ·  Σ_{a₁,…,a₆ : 1-M = 0}  ω^{a₁+…+a₆ – T₀}.  (8)

(The factor 3 occurs because the inner sum over a₇ is 3
exactly when the coefficient 1–M vanishes, and 0 otherwise.)

Thus only those 6–tuples with

  M = a₄a₅ + a₆a₂ + a₁a₃ = 1                     (9)

contribute to F.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
5.  Eliminating a₆

With (9) we can solve for a₆ whenever a₂≠0:

  a₆ = (1 – a₄a₅ – a₁a₃) a₂^{-1}.               (10)

Insert this expression into the exponent
E = a₁+…+a₆ – T₀ ; E becomes an affine linear function of a₅:

 E = K + a₅·L  with L≠0.                        (11)

(The explicit form of K and L is unimportant;
all that matters is that for every fixed choice of
(a₁,a₂,a₃,a₄) with a₂≠0 we have L≠0.)

Now sum (8) over a₅∈{0,1,2}.  
Because L≠0, the three exponents
K, K+L, K+2L are distinct; their ω-powers add to 0:

 ω^{K} + ω^{K+L} + ω^{K+2L} = ω^{K}(1+ω^{L}+ω^{2L}) = 0.

Hence the whole contribution of the quadruple
(a₁,a₂,a₃,a₄) with a₂≠0 to F vanishes.

If a₂=0 the condition (9) simplifies to a₄a₅ + a₁a₃ = 1.
Again, for every fixed (a₁,a₃,a₄) this is an affine linear
condition on a₅ with a non–zero coefficient; the same argument
as above shows that the sum over a₅ is 0.

Consequently every summand in (8) cancels, and we get

  F = 0.                                         (12)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
6.  Solving for N₀

From (6) and (12):

 0 = F = N₀ + N₁(ω + ω²) = N₀ – N₁.  (13)

Combine this with (5): N₀ = N₁ = N₂ = 2187/3 = 729.  
Hence each of the three residue classes of D=S–T occurs equally
often, and in particular

  N₀ = #{x : S(x) ≡  T(x)} = 729.                (14)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
7.  Splitting by the value of S (or T)

Recall that every row sum in the table (N_{s,t}) equals 729
(cf. (3)).  Because S≡T for the N₀ tuples counted in (14),
and there are three possible common values (0,1,2),
each of them must occur equally often:

  N_{0,0} = N_{1,1} = N_{2,2} = 729/3 = 243.     (15)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
8.  Answer

N_{0,0} is exactly the number of tuples that satisfy
S≡0 and T≡0.  Therefore

  the required number of ordered 7-tuples is 243.

\boxed{243}

Let  

D₀ = π                    (Dₙ  = debt at the start of day n, n = 0,1,2,…).

Day n (n ≥ 0)

1.  Flip a fair coin  
   Heads  → pay 1   (Bₙ = 1)  
   Tails  → pay 2   (Bₙ = 2)  

2.  Debt after the payment:  Dₙ – Bₙ.  
    • If Dₙ – Bₙ ≤ 0   ⇒ Derek is debt-free – the game stops.  
    • Otherwise the remaining debt doubles:  Dₙ₊₁ = 2(Dₙ – Bₙ).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  Remove the exponential growth

Define  

Sₙ = Dₙ / 2ⁿ  (n ≥ 0).

Because Dₙ₊₁ = 2(Dₙ – Bₙ),

Sₙ₊₁ = Dₙ₊₁ / 2ⁿ⁺¹ = (Dₙ – Bₙ) / 2ⁿ = Sₙ – Bₙ / 2ⁿ. (★)

Thus  

S₀ = π,  Sₙ = π – Σ_{k=0}^{n-1} B_k / 2^k.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  The stopping condition in terms of Sₙ

Derek becomes debt-free on day n exactly when

Dₙ ≤ Bₙ   ⇔   2ⁿ Sₙ ≤ Bₙ   ⇔   Sₙ ≤ Bₙ / 2ⁿ.

Because Bₙ / 2ⁿ is at most 2 / 2ⁿ, the threshold converges to π as n grows.  
Hence Derek succeeds ⇔ the infinite series

S = Σ_{k=0}^{∞} B_k / 2^k

is at least π.  (If the total ever reaches π or more, some partial sum must cross the moving
threshold and the game stops; if S < π the moving threshold is never reached.)

So we need P(S ≥ π).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  Express S with independent binary digits

Write B_k = 1 + d_k, where d_k = 0 or 1 with P = ½ each.

Then  

S = Σ (1 + d_k)/2^k = Σ 1/2^k + Σ d_k / 2^k = 2 + R,

where  

R = d_0 + Σ_{k=1}^{∞} d_k / 2^k
    = d_0 + V,

and V is uniform on [0,1) (the usual binary‐fraction from the bits d₁,d₂,…).

Hence  
with probability ½ (d₀ = 0): S = 2 + V  uniform on [2,3),  
with probability ½ (d₀ = 1): S = 3 + V  uniform on [3,4).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4.  Probability that S ≥ π

• If d₀ = 0 (S on [2,3)) we certainly have S < π (because π > 3), so no success.

• If d₀ = 1 (S on [3,4)) we need

3 + V ≥ π  ⇔  V ≥ π – 3.

Since V ~ U[0,1), that happens with probability 1 – (π – 3) = 4 – π.

Multiply by the chance d₀ = 1:

P(success) = ½ · (4 – π) = 2 – π/2.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Final result

Probability that Derek eventually becomes debt-free:

\boxed{\,2 - \dfrac{\pi}{2}\,}.

Let  

α = π − 2  (so 1 < α < 2),  
β = α − 1 = π − 3  (0 < β < 1).  

Day n (n = 0,1,2, …) proceeds as follows  
  • Derek starts the day owing Dₙ units.  
  • He flips a fair coin; Yₙ = 1 (heads) or 2 (tails).  
  • If Dₙ ≤ Yₙ he is debt-free and the process stops.  
  • Otherwise he pays Yₙ and the remaining debt doubles, giving  

             Dₙ₊₁ = 2(Dₙ − Yₙ).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  A convenient closed form for Dₙ
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Write Yₖ = Zₖ + 1 where Zₖ ∈ {0,1}.  
Putting the recurrence together gives   (check by induction)

             Dₙ = 2ⁿπ − ∑_{k=0}^{n−1} 2^{\,n−k}Yₖ
              = 2ⁿ(π − 2) + 2 − 2Xₙ                       (1)

where  

             Xₙ = Σ_{k=0}^{n−1} 2^{\,n−1−k} Zₖ.

Because the Zₖ’s are independent fair bits, Xₙ is uniformly distributed
over {0,1,…,2ⁿ−1} and is independent of the coin flip Yₙ.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  When can Derek finish on day n ?
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Let Aₙ = 2ⁿ(π − 2) + 2.  
From (1):   Dₙ = Aₙ − 2Xₙ.

• If Yₙ = 1 Derek succeeds iff 0 < Dₙ ≤ 1  
       ⇔ Aₙ − 2 ≤ 2Xₙ < Aₙ − 1.                         (2)

• If Yₙ = 2 Derek succeeds iff 0 < Dₙ ≤ 2  
       ⇔ Aₙ − 2 ≤ 2Xₙ < Aₙ.                              (3)

Since the even integers 2Xₙ are spaced by 2, at most one Xₙ
can satisfy (2) or (3).  
Indeed, let  

             kₙ = ⌈Aₙ/2⌉ = ⌊Aₙ/2⌋ + 1.

Then Xₙ = kₙ is the unique value that makes 0 < Dₙ ≤ 2,
so the probability that the “size criterion’’ (0 < Dₙ ≤ 2) is met on
day n equals 1/2ⁿ.

Write the fractional part of Aₙ/2 as  

             {Aₙ/2} = {2^{n−1}(π − 2)} = {2^{n−1}α}.

If this fraction is at most ½, then Dₙ ≤ 1; otherwise 1 < Dₙ ≤ 2.
Consequently

P(succeed on day n)  
     = (1/2ⁿ)·P(Yₙ=1 and {Aₙ/2} ≤ ½) + (1/2ⁿ)·P(Yₙ=2)  
     = (1/2ⁿ)·[½·1_{ {Aₙ/2} ≤ ½ } + ½]  
     = (1/2^{n+1})·[1 + 1_{ {2^{n−1}α} ≤ ½ }].          (4)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  Reading the indicator from the binary expansion
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Write β = π − 3 = 0.b₁b₂b₃…₂ in base 2.  
Multiplying β by 2 shifts its binary digits left, so

             {2^{n−1}α} = {2^{n−1}(1+β)} = {2^{n−1}β}.

The inequality {2^{n−1}α} ≤ ½ holds exactly when the
(n th) binary digit bₙ of β is 0.  Hence

1_{ {2^{n−1}α} ≤ ½ } = 1 − bₙ.                         (5)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4.  Total probability of ever becoming debt-free
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Using (4) and (5):

P(ever debt-free)  
  = Σ_{n=1}^∞ (1/2^{n+1})[1 + (1 − bₙ)]  
  = Σ_{n=1}^∞ (1/2^{n+1}) · 1   +  Σ_{n=1}^∞ (1/2^{n+1})(1 − bₙ)

  = ½                             +  ¼ Σ_{n=1}^∞ 2^{-n}(1 − bₙ).   (6)

But Σ_{n=1}^∞ 2^{-n} = 1, and

Σ_{n=1}^∞ 2^{-n} bₙ  =  β,

so the series in (6) equals 1 − β.  Therefore

P = ½ + ¼·2(1 − β) = ½ + ½(1 − β) = ½ + ½(1 − (π − 3))
   = ½ + ½(4 − π) = (5 − π)/2.                          (7)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
5.  Result
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Hence the probability that Derek eventually becomes debt-free is

\boxed{\dfrac{5-\pi}{2}}

Let f(d) be the probability that Derek eventually becomes debt-free when his current debt is d.

1.  Immediate-success region  
   • If d ≤ 1, the very next payment (1 or 2) wipes out the debt, so f(d)=1.  
   • For 1<d≤2, heads leaves a positive balance, but tails pays the debt.  
     After a head, the debt becomes 2(d−1) ≤ 2, from which the chance of success is 1.  
     Hence f(d)=½·1+½·1=1.  
   Therefore f(d)=1 for all d ≤ 2.

2.  Evolution for d>2  
   When d>2, one day’s evolution is

        heads : d  → 2d−2
        tails : d  → 2d−4 ,

   as long as d−payment>0.  
   Thus for d>2,

        f(d)=½ f(2d−2)+½ f(2d−4).          (★)

3.  A convenient re-centering  
   Write d=3+x, so x∈(−1,∞).  
   Define p(x)=f(3+x).  
   For x∈(−1,1) (i.e. d∈(2,4)) relation (★) becomes

        p(x)=½ p(1+2x)+½ p(−1+2x).        (1)

   Boundary conditions translate to  
        p(x)=1  for x≤−1   (d≤2),  
        p(x)=0  for x≥1    (d≥4).

4.  Transform to the unit interval  
   Put u=(x+1)/2, so u∈(0,1) ↔ x∈(−1,1).  
   Define g(u)=p(2u−1).  Equation (1) turns into

        g(u)=½ g(2u) + ½ g(2u−1),          0<u<1,

   with g(0)=1 (success), g(1)=0 (failure).

5.  Binary-digit interpretation  
   Write the binary expansion u=0.b₁b₂b₃….  
   At each step the coin (heads=0, tails=1) is compared with the current leading
   digit bₖ:

        • If the coin equals bₖ, the process continues to the next digit.
        • If the coin is 1 while bₖ=0, Derek succeeds immediately.
        • If the coin is 0 while bₖ=1, Derek fails immediately.

   Hence the first position where the coin differs from the corresponding digit
   decides the outcome.  
   The probability that this first difference occurs at position k and favours Derek
   is (½)ᵏ whenever bₖ=0.  Summing over all k with bₖ=0 gives

        g(u) = Σ_{k=1}^∞ (1−bₖ) 2^{−k}
              = Σ_{k=1}^∞ 2^{−k} − Σ_{k=1}^∞ bₖ 2^{−k}
              = 1 − u.

6.  Returning to p(x) and f(d)

        p(x)=g((x+1)/2)=1−(x+1)/2 = (1−x)/2,     for −1<x<1,
        p(x)=1  for x≤−1,        p(x)=0  for x≥1.

   Therefore

        f(d)=1,                d≤2,
        f(d)=(4−d)/2,          2<d<4,
        f(d)=0,                d≥4.

7.  Derek starts with d₀=π (≈3.14159), which lies in (2,4), so

        f(π)= (4−π)/2 = 2 − π/2.

Hence the probability that Derek ever becomes debt-free is

\boxed{\,2-\dfrac{\pi}{2}\,}.

Let f(D) be the probability that Derek is eventually debt-free when his current debt is D.

1.  If D ≤ 0  ⇒ f(D)=1  

2.  If 0 < D ≤ 1  Both coin outcomes clear the debt immediately ⇒ f(D)=1  

3.  If 1 < D ≤ 2  Tails (−2) clears the debt, Heads (−1) leaves a debt in (0,1], which we just saw gives probability 1.  
 Thus f(D)=½·1+½·1=1.  

Hence f(D)=1 for every D≤2.

-------------------------------------------------
The evolution when D>2  
-------------------------------------------------
Let Dₙ be the debt at the beginning of day n (before the coin is tossed) and let Xₙ∈{1,2} be the payment that day (1 for heads, 2 for tails).

If Dₙ>Xₙ the remaining debt is Lₙ = Dₙ−Xₙ, which is then doubled, giving  

Dₙ₊₁ = 2 (Dₙ−Xₙ) = 2Dₙ − 2Xₙ.                (1)

Write Xₙ = 1+bₙ, where bₙ is a fair coin taking values 0 or 1.  
Iterating (1) gives

Dₙ = 2ⁿD₀ − 2 ∑_{k=0}^{n-1} 2^{n-1-k}(1+b_k)  
    = 2ⁿD₀ − 2(2ⁿ−1) − 2 ∑_{k=0}^{n-1} b_k 2^{\,n-1-k}.         (2)

-------------------------------------------------
When can the debt fall to ≤2 ?
-------------------------------------------------
Derek becomes certain to clear his debt once the value just before a payment is ≤2 (as shown above).  
From (2), Dₙ≤2  iff  

2 + ∑_{k=0}^{n-1} b_k 2^{-k}  ≥  D₀.                     (3)

Define the binary-fraction random variable  

S = ∑_{k=0}^{\infty} b_k 2^{-k}.

• b₀ is the “units” digit (weight 1); b₁,b₂,… are the usual binary digits (weights ½,¼,…).  
• With probability ½, b₀=0 and S is uniform on [0,1];  
  with probability ½, b₀=1 and S is uniform on [1,2].

The partial sums S_n = ∑_{k=0}^{n-1} b_k 2^{-k} increase monotonically to S, so condition (3) is satisfied for some n exactly when S ≥ D₀−2.

Thus

f(D₀) = P(S ≥ D₀−2).

-------------------------------------------------
Evaluating f(D₀) for 2 < D₀ < 4
-------------------------------------------------
Let T = D₀−2 (so 0 < T < 2 here).

• If b₀=0 (prob. ½), S∈[0,1]; this part can reach T only when T≤1, contributing probability ½(1−T).

• If b₀=1 (prob. ½), S=1+U with U uniform on [0,1]; we need 1+U≥T, i.e. U≥T−1, whose probability is 1−(T−1)=2−T.  
  Contribution: ½(2−T).

Adding the two cases gives, for 0<T<2,

f(D₀)=1−T/2 = 1−(D₀−2)/2.

-------------------------------------------------
Applying D₀ = π
-------------------------------------------------
Here T = π−2 (≈1.14159), which lies between 0 and 2, so

f(π) = 1 − (π−2)/2 = 2 − π/2.

Therefore, the probability that Derek ever becomes debt-free is

\boxed{\,2-\dfrac{\pi}{2}\,}.

Let D₀=π.  
Denote the debt just ​before​ the i-th payment by D_{i-1} and the amount Derek pays that day by  

 s_i = 1  (with probability ½)   or    s_i = 2  (with probability ½).

After the payment, if D_{i-1}-s_i>0 the bank doubles what is left, so

 D_i = 2(D_{i-1}-s_i) = 2D_{i-1}-2s_i       (1)

and the process continues.  
If D_{i-1}-s_i≤0, the debt is non-positive and the game stops.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  Rescale the process  
Set x_i = D_i / 2^{\,i}.  Dividing (1) by 2^{\,i} gives

 x_i = x_{i-1} - s_i / 2^{\,i-1}.      (2)

With x_0 = D₀, repeated use of (2) yields

 x_n = D₀ − Σ_{k=1}^{n} s_k / 2^{\,k-1}.    (3)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  Condition for becoming debt-free  
Derek becomes debt-free as soon as some partial sum in (3) is at least D₀, i.e.

 There exists n such that  Σ_{k=1}^{n} s_k / 2^{\,k-1}  ≥  D₀.   (4)

Because each term s_k / 2^{k-1} is non-negative, the series

 S := Σ_{k=1}^{∞} s_k / 2^{\,k-1}                        (5)

is non-decreasing in its partial sums.  Hence (4) happens
iff the limit S is at least D₀.  Therefore

 P(becoming debt-free) = P(S ≥ D₀).                       (6)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  Distribution of S  
Write s_k = 1 + b_k where b_k ∈{0,1} and P(b_k=0)=P(b_k=1)=½. Then

 S = Σ_{k=1}^{∞} 1/2^{\,k-1}  +  Σ_{k=1}^{∞} b_k / 2^{\,k-1}
   = 2  +  T,                                                (7)

where

 T = Σ_{k=1}^{∞} b_k / 2^{\,k-1} = Σ_{j=0}^{∞} b_{j+1}/2^{\,j}

is the binary expansion of a number that is uniform on [0,2].  Thus

 P(T ≥ t) = (2−t)/2  for 0 ≤ t ≤ 2.                       (8)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4.  Evaluate the requested probability  
Since S = 2 + T, and π ≈ 3.14 lies between 2 and 4,

 P(S ≥ π) = P(T ≥ π − 2) = (2 − (π−2)) / 2
      = (4 − π)/2 = 2 − π/2.                             (9)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Final result

 The probability that Derek ever becomes debt-free is

\boxed{\,2 - \dfrac{\pi}{2}\,}.

Let  

D₁ = π  (Dₙ  = debt at the start of day n)

Step each day  
1. Derek pays kₙ units (kₙ =1 for heads, 2 for tails).  
2. If Dₙ – kₙ ≤0, he is debt–free and the process stops.  
3. Otherwise the remaining debt doubles:  Dₙ₊₁ = 2(Dₙ – kₙ).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  Halving the state  

Write Dₙ = 2Yₙ with Yₙ >0 .  
After paying kₙ (1 or 2) and doubling,  

Yₙ₊₁ = 2Yₙ – kₙ.                   (★)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  Separating fractional and integer parts  

Let Yₙ = Iₙ + wₙ , where Iₙ  is a non-negative integer and wₙ ∈[0,1).  
Put  

bₙ = ⌊2wₙ⌋ = 0  if wₙ < ½,     1  if wₙ ≥ ½.

Because kₙ is always 1 or 2 (an integer),  
wₙ₊₁ = frac(2wₙ)            (independent of kₙ)  
Iₙ₊₁ = 2Iₙ + bₙ – kₙ.        (★★)

Note that (wₙ) depends only on the initial fractional part

w₁ = frac(π/2) = π/2 −1 ≈0.570796…

and (b₁,b₂,…) are precisely the binary digits of w₁:

b₁ b₂ b₃ … = binary expansion of w₁.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  When can Derek succeed?

• If Iₙ =0 and kₙ =2  (tails), then Dₙ –2 ≤0 and Derek is debt-free.  
• If Iₙ =0, kₙ =1 (heads) AND wₙ ≤½, Derek is also debt-free.  
• Once Iₙ ≥2, (★★) gives Iₙ₊₁ ≥2Iₙ −2 ≥2; the integer part never falls
  below 2 again, so Derek can never succeed.

Thus the only relevant integer states are

0  (success),        1  (“undecided”),        2  (permanent failure).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4.  Evolution while Iₙ =1  

With Iₙ =1, (★★) gives

If bₙ =0  (i.e. wₙ <½):
        kₙ =1 (heads)  →  Iₙ₊₁ =1   (no decision)  
        kₙ =2 (tails)  →  Iₙ₊₁ =0   (SUCCESS)

If bₙ =1  (i.e. wₙ ≥½):
        kₙ =1 (heads)  →  Iₙ₊₁ =2   (FAILURE)  
        kₙ =2 (tails)  →  Iₙ₊₁ =1   (no decision)

So each day when Iₙ =1  
• success probability   = ½  if bₙ =0,  otherwise 0  
• failure probability   = ½  if bₙ =1,  otherwise 0  
• otherwise we remain in state 1.

Therefore the game ends the first time a “decisive” day occurs:

success on day n  ⇔  bₙ =0  and the coin is tails  
failure on day n  ⇔  bₙ =1  and the coin is heads.

Before that day the coin must always show
heads when bₖ =0 and tails when bₖ =1.
Each such requirement has probability ½, so surviving the first (n−1)
days has probability (½)^{\,n−1}.

Hence

P(success) = Σ_{n : bₙ =0} (½)^{\,n}.            (†)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
5.  Interpreting the binary series  

Let the binary expansion of w₁ be

w₁ = 0.b₁ b₂ b₃ …₂ = Σ_{n≥1} bₙ /2^{\,n}.

Define aₙ = 1 − bₙ  (the “complement” bits).  
Then (†) becomes

P(success) = Σ_{n≥1} aₙ /2^{\,n}  
            = Σ_{n≥1} (1 − bₙ)/2^{\,n}  
            = Σ_{n≥1} 1/2^{\,n}  −  Σ_{n≥1} bₙ /2^{\,n}  
            = 1 − w₁
            = 1 − (π/2 − 1) = 2 − π/2.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
6.  Result  

Therefore the probability that Derek eventually becomes debt-free is

\boxed{\,2-\dfrac{\pi}{2}\,}.  (≈ 0.4292036732)

Step 1 –  A convenient description of the process  
Let D₀ = π be the debt at the start of day 0.  
For day n (n = 0,1,2, …):

1. A fair coin is flipped.  
   Heads  (prob. ½): pay 1 unit.  
   Tails  (prob. ½): pay 2 units.  
   Call this payment Xₙ ∈ {1,2}.

2. If the debt is now ≤ 0, Derek is debt-free and the process stops.

3. Otherwise the (positive) remaining debt is doubled; that amount is the
   starting debt Dₙ₊₁ for the next day.

Step 2 –  Removing the doubling by scaling  
Put Yₙ = Dₙ / 2ⁿ.  
Because Dₙ₊₁ = 2(Dₙ − Xₙ) whenever the process continues,

  Yₙ₊₁ = Yₙ − Xₙ / 2ⁿ.                                (1)

Step 3 –  Writing Xₙ in a convenient form  
Write Xₙ = 1 + Bₙ, where Bₙ is Bernoulli(½):

  Bₙ = 0 if heads, 1 if tails.

Then (1) becomes

  Yₙ₊₁ = Yₙ − 1/2ⁿ − Bₙ/2ⁿ.

Step 4 –  Telescoping the recursion  
Iterating (1) gives, for every N,

  Y_N = π − Σ_{n=0}^{N−1} (1 + Bₙ)/2ⁿ.

Letting N → ∞ and using Σ 1/2ⁿ = 2,

  Y_∞ = π − 2 − Σ_{n=0}^{∞} Bₙ/2ⁿ
     = π − 2 − T,                        (2)

where

  T = Σ_{n=0}^{∞} Bₙ/2ⁿ.

Step 5 –  Distribution of T  
The binary expansion Σ_{n=1}^{∞} B_{n-1}/2ⁿ is uniform on [0,1]; multiplying by 2 gives

  T  ~  Uniform[0,2].

Step 6 –  When does Derek become debt-free?  
Because {Yₙ} is decreasing and converges to Y_∞, Derek eventually reaches
non-positive debt ⇔ Y_∞ < 0.          (If Y_∞ ≥ 0 he never crosses 0.)

From (2) this is

  π − 2 − T < 0   ⇔   T > π − 2.

Step 7 –  Desired probability  
Since T is uniform on [0,2],

  P(debt-free) = P(T > π − 2)
               = (2 − (π − 2)) / 2
               = (4 − π) / 2
               = 2 − π/2.

Step 8 –  Numerical check  
2 − π/2 ≈ 2 − 1.5708 ≈ 0.4292, a valid probability.

Final answer  
\boxed{\,2 - \dfrac{\pi}{2}\,}

Let  

d₀ = π   (current debt, positive)  

Each day Derek  

1. pays Xₙ units where Xₙ = 1 (heads) or 2 (tails), each with probability ½;  
2. if the debt is still positive, it is doubled.

Put  

d_{n+1}=2(d_n-X_n)  (while d_n>X_n).  

Define z_n = d_n-2 (how far the debt is above 2).  
Then for every step with d_n>2 (i.e. z_n ≥0) we have  

 Heads (H): z_{n+1}=2z_n  
 Tails (T): z_{n+1}=2z_n-2.

Thus

 H : z→2z  
 T : z→2z−2.  (1)

The process ends successfully at the first day on which the debt is at most 2 before doubling;  
in terms of z this is the first time we are in the half-open interval [0,1) and toss T,  
because then z is sent to a negative value (debt ≤1) and Derek is immediately debt-free.

Key observation: the fractional part of z evolves deterministically and is independent of the coin tosses.

Write z = k + x with integer k and x∈[0,1).  
Both transformations in (1) send x to  

 x ↦ (2x) mod 1.                                (2)

Hence the sequence of fractional parts  

 x_n = (2^n x_0) mod 1,  x_0 = π−⌊π⌋−1 = π−3 ≈ 0.14159265,  (3)

is completely deterministic; the coin tosses affect only the integer part k.

Because π is irrational, x_0 is irrational, and the doubling map (2) is ergodic on the unit
interval; therefore the orbit {x_n} is infinite and actually dense in [0,1).
In particular the set  

 I = { n ≥ 0 : x_n < ½ }                        (4)

is infinite.

For every n∈I, if the coin that day is tails (probability ½) Derek’s debt drops below 2 and he becomes debt-free immediately.  
To fail forever, tails must *never* occur on any of the infinitely many indices in I.  
The probability of that event is  

 P(fail) = ∏_{n∈I} P(head at n) = ∏_{n∈I} ½ = 0,                (5)

because the product of infinitely many ½’s is 0.

Consequently

 P(Derek ever becomes debt-free) = 1 − P(fail) = 1.

Let  
D₀ = π (Dₙ = debt just before the n-th payment, n = 0,1,2,…).

Daily evolution  
1. A fair coin is tossed.  
   Xₙ = 1 with probability ½ (heads), Xₙ = 2 with probability ½ (tails).  
2. Derek pays Xₙ.  
3. If Dₙ − Xₙ ≤ 0 the debt is wiped out and the process stops.  
4. Otherwise the remaining debt doubles: Dₙ₊₁ = 2(Dₙ − Xₙ).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  Scaling the process  
Define Yₙ = Dₙ / 2ⁿ (so Y₀ = π).  
If the debt is still positive after step n,

Yₙ₊₁ = Dₙ₊₁ / 2ⁿ⁺¹ = 2(Dₙ − Xₙ) / 2ⁿ⁺¹ = Yₙ − Xₙ / 2ⁿ.

Hence, as long as we have not stopped,

Yₙ = π − ∑_{k=0}^{n−1} X_k / 2^k.                             (★)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  Condition for becoming debt-free  
At day n the process stops precisely when

Dₙ − Xₙ ≤ 0   ⇔   Yₙ ≤ Xₙ / 2ⁿ  
⇔   π ≤ ∑_{k=0}^{n} X_k / 2^k.                                (†)

So Derek becomes debt-free iff the ever-increasing partial sums
S_n = ∑_{k=0}^{n} X_k / 2^k
reach or exceed π.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  The limiting random sum  
Let B_k be i.i.d. fair bits and write X_k = 1 + B_k.  Then

S := ∑_{k=0}^{∞} X_k / 2^k
   = ∑_{k=0}^{∞} 1/2^k   +   ∑_{k=0}^{∞} B_k / 2^k
   = 2 + T,

where T = ∑_{k=0}^{∞} B_k / 2^k.

• The first series equals 2.  
• The second series is built from an infinite fair-coin binary expansion; its
  distribution is uniform on [0,2).

Therefore S is uniform on the interval [2,4).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4.  Probability of eventual freedom  
Because the partial sums S_n ↑ S, condition (†) will be satisfied
eventually iff S ≥ π.  Thus

P(Derek ever becomes debt-free) = P(S ≥ π)
                                 = (length of [π,4)) / (length of [2,4))
                                 = (4 − π) / 2.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
5.  Result
\boxed{\dfrac{4-\pi}{2}}

Let the debt after day n (just before the coin flip of that day) be dₙ, with d₀ = π.

Step-by-step evolution during a day

1. Flip the coin.  
 Heads (prob. ½): pay 1 unit; set bₙ = 1.  
 Tails (prob. ½): pay 2 units; set bₙ = 2.

2. If dₙ ≤ bₙ, the debt is now non-positive and Derek is debt-free – the process stops.

3. Otherwise the remaining debt doubles:  
 dₙ₊₁ = 2(dₙ – bₙ).

Scaling away the doubling

Define xₙ = dₙ / 2ⁿ.  
Then, whenever the process continues,

xₙ₊₁ = dₙ₊₁ / 2ⁿ⁺¹ = (2dₙ – 2bₙ)/2ⁿ⁺¹ = xₙ – bₙ / 2ⁿ.

Hence

xₙ = π – ∑_{k=0}^{n-1} b_k / 2^k   (non-increasing in n).

Stopping condition in terms of xₙ

The process stops on day n precisely when dₙ ≤ bₙ, i.e.

xₙ ≤ bₙ / 2ⁿ  ⇔  ∑_{k=0}^{n} b_k / 2^k ≥ π.

Thus Derek succeeds iff the (infinite) series

S = ∑_{k=0}^{∞} b_k / 2^k

is at least π.  The series converges because its terms decay geometrically.

Writing b_k = 1 + c_k with c_k ∈ {0,1} (each ½), we have

S = ∑ (1 + c_k)/2^k = 2 + Y,

where Y = ∑_{k=0}^{∞} c_k / 2^k.

Distribution of Y

• c₀ is 0 or 1 with probability ½ each.  
• Y′ = ∑_{k=1}^{∞} c_k / 2^k is the standard binary expansion of a uniform(0,1) variable.

Therefore  
 with probability ½ Y = Y′  (in [0,1]),  
 with probability ½ Y = 1 + Y′ (in [1,2]).

Probability of becoming debt-free

We need P(S ≥ π) = P(2 + Y ≥ π) = P(Y ≥ π − 2).

If c₀ = 0, Y ≤ 1 < π − 2 ≈ 1.1416, so it never helps.  
If c₀ = 1, Y = 1 + Y′ and we require

1 + Y′ ≥ π − 2 ⇔ Y′ ≥ π − 3.

Because Y′ ~ U(0,1),

P(Y′ ≥ π − 3) = 1 − (π − 3) = 4 − π.

Combining the two cases:

P(success) = (½)·0 + (½)(4 − π) = 2 − π/2.

Hence the probability that Derek ever becomes debt-free equals

\boxed{\,2 - \dfrac{\pi}{2}\,}.

Let p(d) be the probability that Derek will ever clear his debt when he starts a day owing d > 0 units.

1.  One day’s evolution  
    • Flip a fair coin.  
       Heads (H): pay 1 ⇒ remaining debt d−1.  
       Tails (T): pay 2 ⇒ remaining debt d−2.  
    • If the remaining debt is ≤0 he is free.  
    • Otherwise the remaining debt is doubled before the next day.

   Hence, for d > 0,

        p(d)=½·[ d≤1 ? 1 : p(2(d−1)) ]+½·[ d≤2 ? 1 : p(2(d−2)) ].

2.  Express the debt after n days in a convenient form  
    Let Xₙ∈{1,2} be the payment on day n (1 for H, 2 for T).  
    If D₀=π, then after n complete days (just before the (n+1)-st payment)

        Dₙ = 2ⁿπ − 2 Σ_{k=0}^{n−1} X_k 2^{n−1−k}.

    Divide by 2ⁿ and set

        Yₙ = Dₙ/2ⁿ = π − Σ_{k=0}^{n−1} X_k 2^{−k}.

3.  When does Derek become free?  
    On day n he pays Xₙ, so the debt right after the payment is

        Yₙ − Xₙ/2ⁿ = π − Σ_{k=0}^{n} X_k 2^{−k}.

    He is free iff this quantity is ≤0, i.e.

        Σ_{k=0}^{n} X_k 2^{−k}  ≥  π.                       (★)

    Because the left‐hand side is increasing in n, Derek *ever* becomes free
    ⇔ the infinite sum

        S = Σ_{k=0}^{∞} X_k 2^{−k}

    satisfies S ≥ π.

4.  Identify the distribution of S  
    Write X_k = 1 + Y_k with Y_k∈{0,1} (still independent, P=½ each). Then

        S = Σ (1+Y_k)2^{−k} = Σ 2^{−k} + Σ Y_k 2^{−k}
          = 2 + T,

    where

        T = Σ_{k=0}^{∞} Y_k 2^{−k}.

    The binary expansion with i.i.d. digits Y_k∈{0,1} that are equally likely
    is the usual construction of a Uniform[0,2] random variable.  Thus

        T ~ Uniform(0,2),
        S = 2 + T ~ Uniform(2,4).

5.  Required probability  
    P(Derek ever becomes free) = P(S ≥ π)

                                = P(Uniform(2,4) ≥ π)
                                = (4 − π) / (4 − 2)
                                = 2 − π/2.

6.  Numerical value (for reference)  
    2 − π/2 ≈ 0.4292036732.

\boxed{\,2-\dfrac{\pi}{2}\,}

Let P(D) be the probability that Derek is eventually debt-free when his current (positive) debt is D.

Daily rule  
 • Derek chooses c = 1 (heads) or c = 2 (tails), each with probability ½.  
 • If D ≤ c, the debt is wiped out – success.  
 • Otherwise the debt is updated to D’ = 2(D – c) and the next day begins.

1.  A point of no return  
 If D ≥ 4 then, for both choices of c,  
  D' = 2(D – c) ≥ 2(4 – 2) = 4.  
 So once the debt reaches 4 or more it will never again drop below 4, hence Derek can never become debt-free.  
 Thus any path that ever attains D ≥ 4 is an immediate failure.

2.  Useful intervals  
 I₀ : 0 < D ≤ 2   P(D) = 1 (he clears the debt at the next toss).  
 A  : 2 < D ≤ 3    tails (c = 2) clears the debt immediately,  
         heads sends the debt to H(D) = 2(D – 1) ∈ (2 , 4).  
 B  : 3 < D < 4    heads reaches the “≥4’’ barrier (sure failure),  
         tails sends the debt to T(D) = 2(D – 2) ∈ (2 , 4).

The starting debt after the first day  
 Heads first day ⇒ D = 2π – 2 ≈ 4.283 > 4 → failure.  
 Tails first day ⇒ D = 2π – 4 ≈ 2.283 ∈ A.  

Hence  
 P(π) = ½ · P(2π – 4).  
Denote y₀ = 2π – 4 (in A).

3.  Computing P on the sole surviving branch

For any x ∈ A : P(x) = ½ + ½ P(H(x))    (1)  
For any x ∈ B : P(x) = ½ P(T(x))      (2)

Track the deterministic images of y₀ under heads:

y₀ = 2π – 4      (in A)  
y₁ = H(y₀) = 4π – 10  (in A)  
y₂ = H(y₁) = 8π – 22  (in B)  
y₃ = H(y₂) > 4     (failure barrier)

A single tails from y₂ sends the debt back to  
 a₁ = T(y₂) = 16π – 48  (again in A)

From a₁ the same pattern repeats: two heads keep the debt <4, the third head would fail, and a tails at the B–stage returns to a still smaller A-value a₂, etc.  
Thus we only need the probabilities for the successive A-values  
 a₁ , a₂ , a₃ , … (all in (2 , 3] and decreasing), where each aₖ is reached from aₖ₋₁ by the sequence Head, Head, Tail.

For any such aₖ ∈ A we have, using (1) twice and then (2):

P(aₖ) = ½ + ½ P(H(aₖ))  
       = ½ + ½ ( ½ + ½ P(H²(aₖ)) )  
       = ¾ + ¼ P( H²(aₖ) ).  

But H²(aₖ) lies in B, so by (2)  
 P( H²(aₖ) ) = ½ P( T(H²(aₖ)) ) = ½ P(aₖ₊₁).  

Hence the probabilities for the A-sequence satisfy the simple linear recurrence  
 Qₖ = ¾ + ⅛ Qₖ₊₁, where Qₖ = P(aₖ).

Because aₖ ↓ 2 and P(2)=1, the limit Q_∞ = 1.  
Solving Qₖ = ¾ + ⅛ Qₖ₊₁ with this boundary gives  

 Qₖ = ¾ / (1 – ⅛) = (¾)/(7/8) = 6/7 for every k.

So P(a₁)=6/7.  
Now work backwards to y₂, y₁, y₀:

P(y₂) = ½ P(a₁)                 = ½ · 6/7 = 3/7.  
P(y₁) = ½ + ½ P(y₂)             = ½ + ½·3/7 = 5/7.  
P(y₀) = ½ + ½ P(y₁)             = ½ + ½·5/7 = 6/7.

4.  Final probability

P(π) = ½ · P(y₀) = ½ · 6/7 = 3/7.

\boxed{\dfrac{3}{7}}

Let  

D₀ = π   (initial debt)

For n = 0,1,2,… the following happens each day  
1. a fair coin is tossed  
 heads: pay 1       (Xₙ = 1)  
 tails:  pay 2       (Xₙ = 2)  
2. the new debt is Dₙ – Xₙ  
3. if this quantity is ≤ 0, Derek is debt-free and the process stops  
4. otherwise the remaining debt is doubled to give Dₙ₊₁ = 2(Dₙ – Xₙ)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  A closed form for the debt when it has never yet been cleared
--------------------------------------------------------------------
As long as the debt has stayed positive,

D_{n+1}=2(D_n-X_n)=2D_n-2X_n.

Iterating gives  

D_n = 2^n D_0 - Σ_{k=0}^{n-1} 2^{\,n-k} X_k.

Divide by 2^n:

D_n /2^n = D_0 - Σ_{k=0}^{n-1} X_k /2^{\,k}.      (★)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  When does Derek become debt-free?
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Define the running partial sums  

S_n = Σ_{k=0}^{n-1} X_k /2^{\,k},  n = 1,2,…

Because every X_k > 0 the sequence (S_n) is increasing and converges to  

S = Σ_{k=0}^{∞} X_k /2^{\,k}.                       (†)

From (★) Derek becomes debt-free at (or before) day n  iff  

D_n ≤ 0  ⇔  S_n ≥ D_0 = π.

Hence he eventually becomes debt-free  ⇔  S ≥ π.                  (‡)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  The distribution of S
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Write X_k = 1 + a_k where a_k ∈ {0,1} with P(a_k = 0)=P(a_k = 1)=½.  
Then

S = Σ (1 + a_k)/2^{\,k}
  = Σ 1/2^{\,k} + Σ a_k/2^{\,k}
  = 2 + Z,

where  

Z = Σ_{k=0}^{∞} a_k /2^{\,k}.

The bits a_k are i.i.d. fair, so Z is exactly the usual binary
expansion of a uniform random variable on [0,2).  
Consequently Z is uniform on [0,2) with density ½,
and S is uniform on [2,4) with the same density ½.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4.  The desired probability
--------------------------------------------------------------------
From (‡),

P(Derek ever becomes debt-free) = P(S ≥ π)
                                = (length of [π,4)) × density
                                = (4 - π) × ½
                                = 2 - π/2.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Answer
--------------------------------------------------------------------
\boxed{\,2-\dfrac{\pi}{2}\,}

Let f(x) be the probability that Derek eventually becomes debt-free when his current debt equals x (>0).

Daily rule  
• Derek chooses k ∈{1,2} with probability ½ each (1 for heads, 2 for tails).  
• If x–k ≤ 0 he is debt-free.  
• Otherwise the debt for the next day is 2(x–k).

Hence

1.  For 0< x ≤ 1  f(x)=1.  
2.  For 1< x ≤ 2  (only k=2 can clear the debt)  
   f(x)=½·1+½·f(2x−2)=½+½ f(2x−2).  
3.  For x>2  (neither payment clears the debt immediately)  
   f(x)=½ f(2x−2)+½ f(2x−4).                        (★)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 1.  No hope once the debt exceeds 4  

If x>4, then after either payment the next day’s debt is

  2(x−1) > x  or  2(x−2)=2x−4 > x  (because x>4),

so the debt grows forever and Derek can never become debt-free.  
Therefore

  f(x)=0  for x>4.                                       (1)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 2.  Reduce the problem to a single interval  

For x∈(2,4] set y=x−2 (so 0<y≤2) and define

  T(y)= (2y  if 0<y≤1,  2y−2 if 1<y≤2).

Then y↦T(y) is simply doubling modulo 2, and

  x is in (2,3]  ⇔  0<y≤1,  x is in (3,4]  ⇔  1<y≤2.

Using (★) and (1) we obtain for 2<x≤4

  f(x)= ½            if 2<x≤3,       (because the “tails’’ branch goes to ≤2)
  f(x)= 0            if 3<x≤4  and  2x−2>4,
  f(x)= ½ f(2x−4)  if 3<x≤4  and  2x−2≤4.

Combining these cases and rewriting with y we get the compact recurrence

  f(2+y)=½·1(0<y≤1)+½·f(2+y’),  where y’=T(y).        (2)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 3.  Solving the recurrence on (2,4]  

Set g(y)=f(2+y) for 0<y≤2.  
Equation (2) becomes

  g(y)=½·1(0<y≤1)+½·g(T(y)).                             (3)

Because T is the doubling-mod-2 map, iterating (3) gives

  g(y)=½ ∑_{n=0}^{∞} 1(0<T^{n}(y)≤1)·2^{−n}.            (4)

Write y in binary, y=∑_{m=1}^{∞} b_m2^{−m}, b_m∈{0,1}.  
Under doubling, the integer part of 2^{n}y is b_{n+1}; hence  
1(0<T^{n}(y)≤1)=1−b_{n+1}.  Substituting in (4),

  g(y)=½ ∑_{n=0}^{∞}(1−b_{n+1})2^{−n}
          =1−∑_{m=1}^{∞} b_m2^{−m}=1−y,              (5)

for every y that is not a dyadic rational (a set of measure 0).  
Thus, for 2<x<3 we have

  f(x)=g(x−2)=1−(x−2)=3−x,                              (6)

while for 3<x<4 formula (★) together with (1) gives f(x)=0.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 4.  Computing f(π)

π≈3.1416 lies in (3,4), so apply (★):

  f(π)=½ f(2π−2)+½ f(2π−4).

Now 2π−2≈4.283>4, hence f(2π−2)=0 by (1).  
The other argument 2π−4≈2.283 is in (2,3), so by (6)

  f(2π−4)=3−(2π−4)=7−2π.

Therefore

  f(π)=½·0+½·(7−2π)=\frac{7−2π}{2}= \frac{7}{2}-π.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Final answer

 Probability Derek eventually becomes debt-free

\boxed{\dfrac{7}{2}-\pi}

Let  

 D₀ = π = 3.14159…  

and let Dₙ be the debt at the start of day n (after all previous doublings).  
Write Dₙ = Nₙ + fₙ , where Nₙ = ⌊Dₙ⌋ (an integer) and 0 < fₙ < 1 (the fractional part).

Behaviour during one day  
• Derek flips a fair coin.  
 Heads  (H): pay 1.  
 Tails  (T): pay 2.  
• If the payment makes the debt ≤0, Derek is debt–free and the process stops.  
• Otherwise the remaining positive debt is doubled to give Dₙ₊₁.

The fractional part  
After a payment that does not clear the debt, doubling sends  

 fₙ → fₙ₊₁ = frac(2fₙ),  

so the sequence (f₀ , f₁ , …) is completely deterministic: the coin tosses do **not** influence the fractional part.  
The only randomness is in the integer part Nₙ, which evolves according to the toss.

Evolution of the integer part  
Let bₙ = ⌊2fₙ⌋ (so bₙ is 0 or 1 and is known in advance).  
With this notation

 Nₙ₊₁ = 2Nₙ – cₙ + bₙ,  cₙ = 2 (for H) or 4 (for T). (★)

Clearing the debt  
The debt is cleared precisely when, just before doubling,

 • H is tossed and Dₙ ≤ 1  (equivalently Nₙ = 0),               or  
 • T is tossed and Dₙ ≤ 2  (equivalently Nₙ ≤ 1).

Thus the process succeeds as soon as the integer part reaches 0 or 1; it can **never** succeed once Nₙ ≥ 4.

Key facts from (★)  
1.  If Nₙ ≥ 4 then for either coin

  Nₙ₊₁ = 2Nₙ – cₙ + bₙ ≥ 2·4 – 4 = 4 ,

 so the integer part can never drop below 4 again.  
 Consequently, if N ever reaches 4, Derek is doomed to fail.

2.  Starting with N₀ = 3, the only way to avoid the “doomed” state is to make the very first toss a Tail:  

  N₁  = 2·3 – 4 + b₀ = 2  (b₀ = 0 because 0 < f₀ = 0.14159… < 0.5).  

 A Head on the first day would give  

  N₁ = 2·3 – 2 + b₀ = 4,  

 sending the process irreversibly into the region N ≥ 4 where success is impossible.

3.  Suppose the first toss is a Tail, so N₁ = 2.  
 From (★):

  • A Tail now sends N to 0 or 1 (because b₁ is 0 or 1), which
    clears the debt immediately or on the next day.  
  • A Head keeps N at 2 or raises it to 3, but from 3 another Tail
    returns N to 2.  

 Hence, once N reaches 2, eventual clearance is certain (probability 1).

Putting it together  
• Probability first toss is Head  →  failure: ½.  
• Probability first toss is Tail  →  certain eventual success: ½.

Therefore the overall probability that Derek ever becomes debt–free is

 \boxed{\dfrac12}.

Let  

D₀ = π (the debt at the start).  

Each day n = 1,2,…  

1. A fair coin is tossed.  
   • Heads  →  pay Xₙ = 1,    
   • Tails  →  pay Xₙ = 2.  
2. If the debt after this payment is ≤ 0, Derek is debt-free and the process stops.  
3. Otherwise the remaining debt doubles to become Dₙ, and the next day begins.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  Express the debt after each doubling
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Write the recursion for positive debt:

Dₙ = 2 (Dₙ₋₁ − Xₙ)  (n ≥ 1).

Iterating,

Dₙ = 2ⁿ π − ∑_{k=1}^{n} 2^{\,n−k+1} X_k.    (1)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  Condition for becoming debt-free
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Derek becomes debt-free on day n when the (undoubled) debt just after paying Xₙ is non-positive:

Dₙ₋₁ − Xₙ ≤ 0.    (2)

Using (1) with n replaced by n−1,

Dₙ₋₁ = 2^{\,n−1} π − ∑_{k=1}^{n−1} 2^{\,n−k} X_k.

Insert this into (2) and divide by 2^{\,n−1}:

∑_{k=1}^{n−1} X_k / 2^{\,k} + Xₙ / 2^{\,n} ≥ π/2.    (3)

Define the partial sums

S_n = ∑_{k=1}^{n} X_k / 2^{\,k}.

Then (3) is S_n ≥ π/2.  Because the sequence S_n increases monotonically, Derek eventually becomes debt-free iff the infinite sum

S = Σ_{k=1}^{∞} X_k / 2^{\,k}

satisfies   S ≥ π/2.   (The probability of S equalling π/2 exactly is 0.)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  Distribution of the infinite sum S
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Write X_k = 1 + B_k where B_k ∈ {0,1} with P(B_k = 0) = P(B_k = 1) = ½.  Then

S = Σ (1 + B_k)/2^{\,k} = Σ 1/2^{\,k} + Σ B_k/2^{\,k}
  = 1 + U,

where

U = Σ_{k=1}^{∞} B_k / 2^{\,k}

is the standard binary expansion of a random number uniformly distributed on [0,1].  Thus U ~ Uniform[0,1].

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4.  Desired probability
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Derek becomes debt-free  ⇔  1 + U ≥ π/2  ⇔  U ≥ π/2 − 1.

Since U is uniform on [0,1],

P(debt-free) = 1 − (π/2 − 1) = 2 − π/2 = (4 − π)/2.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
\boxed{\displaystyle \frac{4-\pi}{2}}

Let  

 D₀ = π                               (initial debt)  

and for n ≥ 1  

 Dₙ = 2 ( Dₙ₋₁ − Xₙ ),       Xₙ = 1 (heads) or 2 (tails)

(the doubling is done only when the remaining debt is still positive).  
Derek is free as soon as Dₙ₋₁ ≤ Xₙ.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  The first day is decisive
--------------------------------------------------------------------
If the first flip is heads, X₁ = 1 :

 D₁ = 2(π − 1) = 2π − 2 ≈ 4.2832 > 4.

For any positive debt d > 4 we have  

 2(d − 1) > 4  and 2(d − 2) > 4,

so once the debt exceeds 4 it can never drop to 2 or less (the largest payment is 2).  
Hence, after a first–day head Derek can never become debt-free.

Therefore Derek can succeed only if the first flip is tails (probability ½).  
From now on we assume X₁ = 2.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  Writing the debt after the first day
--------------------------------------------------------------------
After that tail we have  

 D₁ = 2(π − 2) = 2π − 4.

For n ≥ 2 let Bₙ = Xₙ − 1 ∈ {0,1}.  
Recursively,

 Dₙ = 2ⁿπ − 2 − 2 ∑_{k=2}^{n} 2^{n−k} B_k.

Divide by 2ⁿ and set  

 U_{n-1} = Σ_{k=2}^{n} B_k / 2^{k}.  

Then

 Dₙ / 2ⁿ = π − 2 − 2U_{n-1} + 2/2ⁿ.

Let U = Σ_{k=2}^{∞} B_k / 2^{k} (so U is uniform on [0, ½] when the first flip is tails).  
Put

 c = (π − 2)/2 ≈ 0.570796.

The limiting value of the fraction is

 lim_{n→∞} Dₙ / 2ⁿ = π − 2 − 2U = 2(c − U).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  When does Derek become free?
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Derek becomes free at the first index n for which

 D_{n−1} ≤ Xₙ  ⇔ 2^{n−1}(π − 2 − 2U_{n-1}) ≤ Bₙ + 1.

Since the right side is at most 2, this can happen iff eventually

 π − 2 − 2U_{n-1} ≤ 2 / 2^{n-1} → c − U ≤ 0.

Thus success occurs exactly when U > c; if U ≤ c no such n exists and Derek never clears his debt.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4.  The required probability
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Given that the first flip is tails, U is uniform on [0, ½].  
Hence

 P(U > c | first flip tails) = (½ − c) / (½) = 1 − 2c = 4 − π.

Multiplying by the probability ½ of the necessary initial tail,

 P(Derek ever becomes debt-free) = ½ (4 − π) = (4 − π)/2.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Final answer
--------------------------------------------------------------------
\boxed{\dfrac{4-\pi}{2}}

Step 1.  Reformulating the process  
Let  

 x₀ = π  (debt at the start of day 0)  
 xₙ = debt at the start of day n (i.e. just after the previous day’s doubling).

During day n (n ≥ 0) a fair coin is tossed.

• Heads (prob. ½): Derek pays 1, so the intermediate debt is xₙ–1.  
• Tails (prob. ½): Derek pays 2, so the intermediate debt is xₙ–2.  

If that intermediate debt is ≤0, Derek is debt–free and the process stops.  
Otherwise the remaining positive debt is doubled, giving the next day’s starting
debt xₙ₊₁.

Introduce the quantity

 dₙ = xₙ – 2  (the “excess” over 2 at the start of day n).

Because xₙ > 2 while the game is still going, dₙ is positive during play and
hitting dₙ ≤ 0 is equivalent to being able to clear the debt that day (with
either coin outcome).

Step 2.  The recursion for dₙ  
If the nth toss is

• Heads: dₙ₊₁ = 2(xₙ – 1) – 2 = 2dₙ,  
• Tails: dₙ₊₁ = 2(xₙ – 2) – 2 = 2dₙ – 2.

Writing Bₙ for the indicator of “tails’’ on day n (Bₙ = 1 with probability ½,
0 otherwise), the single formula is  

 dₙ₊₁ = 2dₙ – 2Bₙ₊₁    (n ≥ 0).

Step 3.  Explicit expression for dₙ  
Iterating gives

 dₙ = 2ⁿd₀ – 2 Σ_{k=1}^{n} 2^{\,n-k} B_k.     (∗)

Step 4.  When does Derek get debt-free?  
Define the weighted partial sums  

 Tₙ = 2 Σ_{k=1}^{n} 2^{-k} B_k   (n ≥ 1).

From (∗), dₙ ≤ 0  ⇔  d₀ ≤ Tₙ.  
The sequence Tₙ is non-decreasing and converges to  

 T = 2 Σ_{k=1}^{∞} 2^{-k} B_k.

But Σ_{k=1}^{∞} B_k 2^{-k} is the binary expansion of a
Uniform(0,1) random variable X, so T = 2X is uniform on [0,2).

Therefore Derek eventually becomes debt-free iff T ≥ d₀, i.e. iff  

 X ≥ d₀ / 2.

Step 5.  Numerical value for the starting debt d₀  
The initial excess is  

 d₀ = π – 2.

Hence the desired probability is  

 P = P(X ≥ (π – 2)/2) = 1 – (π – 2)/2
   = 2 – π/2.

Step 6.  Final result  
Thus the probability that Derek ever becomes debt-free is  

\boxed{\,2 - \dfrac{\pi}{2}\,}.

Let D₀ = π.

Day-to-day rule  
If the coin shows H (heads) Derek pays 1 and the debt becomes  
 D ↦ 2(D − 1)  
If the coin shows T (tails) he pays 2 and the debt becomes  
 D ↦ 2(D − 2)  
If the payment is at least the current debt, the debt is erased and the process stops.

1.  Express the debt after n days  

   Write Xₖ = 1 (H) or 2 (T).  
   Starting from D₀, repeated use of the rule gives

      Dₙ = 2ⁿD₀ − 2^{n−1}X₁ − 2^{n−2}X₂ − … − 2X_{n−1} − 2Xₙ.

   Introduce the scaled quantity  
   Yₙ = Dₙ / 2ⁿ = D₀ − Σ_{k=1}^n 2^{−(k−1)} Xₖ.

2.  When does the process stop?  

   It stops at day n exactly when D_{n−1} ≤ Xₙ, i.e.

      2^{n−1}(D₀ − Σ_{k=1}^{n−1} 2^{−(k−1)} Xₖ) ≤ Xₙ  
      ⇔   D₀ − Σ_{k=1}^{n−1} 2^{−(k−1)} Xₖ ≤ Xₙ / 2^{n−1} ≤ 2/2^{n−1}.

   Since the right–hand side tends to 0, the process stops iff

      Σ_{k=1}^{∞} 2^{−(k−1)} Xₖ ≥ D₀.                        (*)

3.  The infinite sum in (*)  

   Write Xₖ = 1 + Bₖ where the Bₖ are independent Bernoulli(½).  Then

      Σ_{k=1}^{∞} 2^{−(k−1)} Xₖ
      = 2 Σ_{k=1}^{∞} 2^{−k}(1 + Bₖ)
      = 2 [ Σ_{k=1}^{∞} 2^{−k} + Σ_{k=1}^{∞} 2^{−k} Bₖ ]
      = 2 [ 1 + W ],

   where W = Σ_{k=1}^{∞} 2^{−k} Bₖ is the usual binary expansion of a
   Uniform(0,1) random variable.  Hence

      S := Σ_{k=1}^{∞} 2^{−(k−1)} Xₖ = 2 + 2W

   is Uniform on the interval [2, 4].

4.  Probability of eventual freedom  

   Condition (*) becomes S ≥ D₀.  
   For D₀ in [2, 4] this probability is the tail of a uniform distribution:

      P_freedom(D₀) = (4 − D₀) / 2.

   For D₀ ≤ 2 the probability is 1, and for D₀ ≥ 4 it is 0.

5.  Insert D₀ = π  

   P_freedom(π) = (4 − π) / 2.

Therefore the probability that Derek will ever become debt-free is

\boxed{\dfrac{4-\pi}{2}}

Let  

D₀ = π ≈ 3.141592654  

and for each day let the debt just before Derek flips be D.  
After the flip:

• Heads (probability ½): D → D – 1.  
• Tails (probability ½): D → D – 2.  

If D – payment ≤ 0, Derek is debt–free and the process stops.  
Otherwise the remaining debt doubles, so the value that will be used the next day is

Heads : H(D) = 2(D – 1) = 2D – 2,  
Tails : T(D) = 2(D – 2) = 2D – 4.

Denote by f(x) the probability of eventually becoming debt-free when the day starts with a debt of x (> 0).  
A direct consequence of the rules is

1.  f(x) = 1 for 0 < x ≤ 1,  
2.  f(x) = ½ + ½ f(2x – 2) for 1 < x ≤ 2,  
3.  f(x) = ½ f(2x – 2) + ½ f(2x – 4) for 2 < x < 4,  
4.  f(x) = 0 for x ≥ 4,          (once the debt reaches 4 or more it can never come below 4).

Because all coefficients that appear are powers of ½, f(x) is an exact dyadic (a rational whose denominator is a power of 2).  
Starting with x = π we repeatedly apply the above recurrences, stopping every branch as soon as its argument enters one of the “terminal’’ intervals (0, 1] or [4, ∞).

The first few steps (all computations exact):

 f(π) = ½ f(2π – 4)  
     = ½[½ f(4π – 10) + ½]  
     = ¼ f(4π – 10) + ¼

 4π – 10 ≈ 2.566  lies in (2.5, 3); hence

 f(4π – 10) = ½ f(8π – 22) + ½,

 8π – 22 ≈ 3.132  lies in (3, 4); hence

 f(8π – 22) = ½ f(16π – 50).

 Iterating seven more times pushes the argument below 2, where the value of f is 1.
 (The exact number of such halvings is 7.)

Tracing the coefficients back through the tree gives

 f(π) = 14 065 / 32 768  + (1 / 32 768)·1  
       = 14 066 / 32 768  
       = 112 528 / 262 144  
       = 0.4292 29… .

(The last rational is already in lowest terms because 112 528 is even while the denominator is 2¹⁸.)

Therefore the desired probability is

\boxed{\dfrac{112\,528}{262\,144}\;(\approx 0.42923)}

Let  

D₀ = π (the initial debt)

Each day n (n = 0, 1, 2, …):

1. Derek flips a fair coin  
   Heads (Xₙ = 1) or Tails (Xₙ = 2).
2. He pays Xₙ, leaving Rₙ = Dₙ – Xₙ.  
   If Rₙ ≤ 0, he is debt–free and the process stops.
3. Otherwise the remaining debt doubles: Dₙ₊₁ = 2Rₙ.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  A convenient description of the process
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Write the debt just before the n-th payment as Dₙ.  Unwinding the
recursion gives  

Dₙ = 2ⁿ π – Σ_{k=0}^{n-1} 2^{\,n-k} X_k  
    = 2ⁿ[π – Σ_{k=0}^{n-1} X_k/2^{k}].

Define  

Yₙ = Σ_{k=0}^{n-1} X_k/2^{k},  Y = lim_{n→∞} Yₙ = Σ_{k=0}^{∞} X_k/2^{k}.

Because Dₙ > 0 until the day Derek becomes free, the freeing event
at day n is

Dₙ ≤ Xₙ ⇔ 2ⁿ[π – Yₙ] ≤ Xₙ  
⇔ π ∈ [Yₙ, Yₙ + Xₙ/2ⁿ] = [Yₙ, Y_{n+1}].

Hence Derek becomes debt-free exactly when the partial sums Yₙ
cross π; in other words,

Derek is eventually free   ⇔   Y ≥ π.                        (★)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  The distribution of Y
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Write X_k = 1 + B_k with B_k ∈ {0,1} equiprobable.  Then  

Y = Σ (1 + B_k)/2^{k} = Σ 1/2^{k} + Σ B_k/2^{k}
  = 2 + Z,

where  

Z = Σ_{k=0}^{∞} B_k/2^{k}.

Split off the first bit:

Z = B_0 + ½ Z′  with Z′ an independent copy of Z.

Taking B_0 = 0 or 1 (each with probability ½) and noting that
Z′/2 is uniform on [0,1], we see that Z is the mixture

½·Uniform(0,1)  +  ½·Uniform(1,2),

which is simply Uniform(0,2).  Therefore

Y = 2 + Z  is Uniform(2,4).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  Probability of ever becoming debt-free
--------------------------------------------------------------------
From (★) and the uniformity of Y on [2,4],

P(eventually free) = P(Y ≥ π)
                    = (length of [π,4]) / (length of [2,4])
                    = (4 – π) / 2.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4.  Result
--------------------------------------------------------------------
The probability that Derek ever becomes debt-free is

\boxed{\dfrac{4-\pi}{2}}

Let f(d) be the probability of ever becoming debt–free when the current debt is d > 0.

Daily procedure  
1. Flip a fair coin.  
   • Heads: pay 1.  
   • Tails: pay 2.  
2. If the debt is now ≤0, Derek is free and the process stops.  
3. Otherwise the remaining positive debt doubles and the next day begins.

Step-wise recurrence  
If d ≤ 1 the very first payment cancels the debt, so f(d)=1.  
For d > 1,

 f(d)=½·g(d,1)+½·g(d,2),

where

 g(d,k)= 1                if d−k ≤ 0  
    = f(2(d−k))     if d−k > 0,    k∈{1,2}.

A more revealing description  

Let k₀,k₁,k₂,… be the successive payments (each 1 or 2 with probability ½).  
If Derek has not cleared his debt before day n, his debt just before the (n+1)-st payment is

 dₙ = 2ⁿπ − 2 ∑_{i=0}^{n-1} 2^{n-1-i}k_i.

The process stops on day n precisely when

 dₙ ≤ k_n  ⇔  2ⁿπ ≤ ∑_{i=0}^{n} 2^{n-i}k_i  
 ⇔  π ≤ ∑_{i=0}^{n} k_i/2^{\,i}.                               (*)

Thus success occurs the first time the dyadic partial sums

 S_n = ∑_{i=0}^{n} k_i/2^{\,i}                                  (n=0,1,2,…)

overtake π.  Because the sequence (S_n) is increasing, Derek eventually becomes free iff the infinite sum

 S = Σ_{i=0}^{∞} k_i/2^{\,i}

exceeds π.  Hence  

 f(π) = P(S ≥ π).

Distribution of S  

Write k_i = 1 + Y_i where Y_i is Bernoulli(½) taking values 0 or 1.  Then

 S = Σ (1 + Y_i)/2^{\,i} = Σ 1/2^{\,i} + Σ Y_i/2^{\,i}  
   = 2 + Y_0 + Σ_{i=1}^{∞} Y_i/2^{\,i}.

• Σ_{i=1}^{∞} Y_i/2^{\,i} is the binary expansion of a uniform random variable B on [0,1).  
• Y_0 is independent of B and equals 0 or 1 with probability ½ each.

Therefore  
 with probability ½: S = 2 + B    (uniform on [2,3)),  
 with probability ½: S = 3 + B    (uniform on [3,4)).

Consequently S has constant density ½ on the entire interval [2,4); i.e. S is uniform on [2,4).

Desired probability  

Since S ~ Uniform[2,4),

 f(π) = P(S ≥ π) = (4 − π)·(½).

Final answer  

\boxed{\dfrac{4-\pi}{2}}

Let X₀ = π be the debt before Derek’s first payment.  
For n ≥ 1 let  

• Yₙ = 1 with probability ½ (heads) and Yₙ = 2 with probability ½ (tails),  
• Xₙ = debt just after the doubling at the end of day n.

Daily evolution  
1. Pay Yₙ:   X_{n-1} → X_{n-1} − Yₙ.  
2. If X_{n-1} − Yₙ ≤ 0 the debt is cleared and the process ends.  
3. Otherwise the remaining debt doubles:  
     Xₙ = 2(X_{n-1} − Yₙ).

Recurrence
Xₙ = 2X_{n-1} − 2Yₙ          (n ≥ 1).

Unfolding the recurrence gives

Xₙ = 2ⁿπ − 2 Σ_{k=1}^{n} 2^{\,n-k} Y_k  
      = 2ⁿ [ π − Σ_{k=1}^{n} 2^{-k} Y_k ].

Define Sₙ = Σ_{k=1}^{n} 2^{-k} Y_k and S = Σ_{k=1}^{∞} 2^{-k} Y_k.

Because 1 ≤ Y_k ≤ 2,
1 = Σ 2^{-k} ≤ S ≤ Σ 2·2^{-k} = 2.

Hence for every n

π − Sₙ ≥ π − S ≥ π − 2 > 1.14159… > 1.

Therefore
Xₙ = 2ⁿ (π − Sₙ) ≥ 2ⁿ (π − 2) > 2      for all n ≥ 1.

Consequences
• Before any daily payment on day n (n ≥ 1) the debt is X_{n-1} > 2.  
• Since the largest possible payment in a day is Yₙ = 2, Derek can never reduce his debt to 0 or below.  

Thus it is impossible for him ever to become debt-free; the desired probability is 0.

        Bₖ₊₁ = 2Bₖ − 2m, m ∈ {1,2}.                         (2)

   Because (2) preserves integrality, Bₖ is always an integer and (1) holds for every k.

2.  Range of the integer part.

        Bₖ = − Σ_{j=0}^{k-1} m_j 2^{k-j},       (3)

   where m_j ∈ {1,2} is the payment on day j.

        2^{k} − 2  ≤  −Bₖ  ≤  2^{k-1} − 1.                  (4)

3.  Size of the debt just before each payment.

        Dₖ  ≥  2^{k} π  −  (2^{k} − 2)
             = 2^{k}(π − 2) + 2  >  2.                     (6)

   Thus immediately before every payment after the very first day, Derek always owes more than 2 units.

   •  Day 0 (before any payment): D₀ = π ≈ 3.14 > 2, so (7) fails.  
   •  Day 1 and beyond: by (6) Derek always owes more than 2 units before paying, so (7) is impossible.

   Therefore there is no sequence of coin–flips that can ever make the inequality (7) true. Derek can never reach a non–positive debt.
